candidate_policy: Skip rows with blocked_by until ready_when is satisfied.
Skip slice_size=umbrella rows until they are split.

Default cmd/builder-loop CLI runs cap eligible roadmap work at Phase 4 unless MAX_PHASE is explicitly overridden; the production service unit sets MAX_PHASE=0 so the planner-builder loop can keep advancing across phases.
MAX_AGENTS is a safety cap: if fewer metadata-ready rows are available, run fewer workers instead of selecting filler or random work.
Each worker runs in an isolated git worktree under RUN_ROOT/worktrees and promotion rejects committed paths outside the selected row's write_scope.

When git worktrees are available and MAX_AGENTS is greater than 1, cmd/builder-loop launches selected workers concurrently, then validates and promotes each branch through the same ledgered safety gates.

When PRE_PROMOTION_VERIFY_COMMANDS is configured, cmd/builder-loop verifies the worker branch before cherry-picking, repairs failures on that worker branch, and keeps main untouched until the gate passes.
After all promotions, cmd/builder-loop runs the mandatory post-promotion full-suite gate before emitting run_completed or health_updated.
On post-promotion gate failure, cmd/builder-loop starts one backend repair attempt by default, requires the checkout to be clean, reruns the suite, and records final health only if the gate passes.
Backend worker failures preserve captured stderr/stdout detail in the ledger so repair and planner loops can diagnose the real failure instead of only seeing backend_failed.
Prefer contract rows with write_scope, test_commands, and done_signal.
Inject selected progress metadata into the worker prompt instead of asking workers to rescan the whole roadmap.

items.name: Orchestrator failure-row stabilization for 4-8 workers

Non-timeout/non-OOM codex exits can become soft_success_nonzero only after final-report and commit verification pass.
Timeout and OOM exits remain hard failures.

items.name: Autoloop row health and quarantine contract
items.contract: Autoloop records per-row health, failure categories, quarantine state, stale-spec recovery, and backend penalties without letting planner writes mutate runtime-owned health blocks
items.degraded_mode: Rows with repeated worker failures are skipped or demoted with visible health/quarantine evidence instead of being retried indefinitely.
ready_when: RowHealth is a typed progress field and autoloop can batch health updates after worker outcomes.
not_ready_when: The planner modifies or removes existing health blocks instead of treating them as autoloop-owned runtime state.
acceptance: RowHealth, Quarantine, and ItemSpecHash round-trip through the progress model.
Autoloop writes success/failure health updates, quarantines rows after threshold failures, clears stale quarantines after spec changes, and emits trigger events.
Candidate selection skips active quarantines by default, exposes stale quarantines for reset, and applies deterministic failure penalties.
items.note: Complete: internal/progress now owns RowHealth plus ItemSpecHash and ApplyHealthUpdates; internal/builderloop writes batched run health, quarantines repeated failures with current spec hashes, clears stale quarantines, emits planner trigger events, filters active quarantines, and demotes rows with repeated failures/backends tried.
done_signal: Health writer, candidate selection, and progress preservation tests prove quarantine state is runtime-owned, typed, and stale-spec recoverable.

provenance.note: Autoloop row health/quarantine is a Gormes control-plane invention with no upstream Hermes, Honcho, or GBrain implementation analog.

items.name: Planner self-healing verdict loop
items.contract: Architecture planner consumes autoloop health/ledger outcomes, stamps planner-owned verdicts, retries validation-safe regenerations, and escalates rows that stay failing after repeated reshapes
items.degraded_mode: Rows that resist planner reshapes are marked needs_human and removed from default autoloop selection instead of silently cycling.
ready_when: Autoloop row health blocks and planner trigger events exist on main.
not_ready_when: The slice relies on live LLM calls or manual ledger edits instead of deterministic planner/autoloop fixtures.
acceptance: PlannerVerdict round-trips and is preserved by autoloop health writes.
Planner self-evaluation distinguishes unstuck, still_failing, and no_attempts_yet outcomes.
NeedsHuman is sticky after the configured reshape threshold, and autoloop skips those rows by default.
items.note: Complete: architectureplanner now records planner ledger events, consumes trigger/cursor state, evaluates previous reshapes against autoloop outcomes, retries validation failures with feedback, stamps PlannerVerdict reshape counts/outcomes, escalates persistent failures to needs_human, and exposes needs-human rows in status while autoloop skips them by default.
done_signal: Planner lifecycle tests prove trigger consumption, reshape self-evaluation, PlannerVerdict stamping, sticky needs_human escalation, and autoloop candidate skipping.

provenance.note: PlannerVerdict, planner retry/self-evaluation, and needs_human selection are Gormes-owned autoloop control-plane behavior.

Implementation inventory lists Gormes-original paths under cmd/builder-loop, cmd/planner-loop, internal/builderloop, internal/plannerloop, internal/plannertriggers, and progress health schema.
Status output buckets drift states and records forward-only drift promotions.

items.name: Watchdog checkpoint coalescing
items.contract: Pure helper internal/builderloop/watchdog_coalesce.go exposes type Decision string with constants DecisionFirst="first", DecisionAmend="amend", DecisionNoop="noop"; type CheckpointState struct{LastCheckpointAt time.Time; LastSubject string; WindowID string}; type CoalesceConfig struct{WindowSeconds int; Dirty bool; NextWindowID func() string} and one function DecideCheckpoint(now time.Time, st CheckpointState, cfg CoalesceConfig) (Decision, CheckpointState). Window math: windowSeconds <= 0 falls back to 600. Algorithm: when cfg.Dirty is false, return DecisionNoop and the input state unchanged. When cfg.Dirty is true and (st.WindowID == "" OR now.Sub(st.LastCheckpointAt) >= window), return DecisionFirst with state {LastCheckpointAt: now, LastSubject: st.LastSubject (caller fills), WindowID: cfg.NextWindowID()}. Otherwise (cfg.Dirty and inside the window) return DecisionAmend with state {LastCheckpointAt: now, LastSubject: st.LastSubject, WindowID: st.WindowID} (preserve the existing WindowID). No git invocation, no shell-script change, no live filesystem mutation in this slice; cfg.NextWindowID is the only source of new IDs and is supplied by the caller (typically a small monotonic counter or a uuid stub in tests).
items.degraded_mode: Watchdog status reports checkpoint_coalesce_active, checkpoint_coalesce_window_seconds, and the existing dirty-worktree checkpoint commit ID instead of emitting a fresh commit on every tick.
ready_when: Watchdog dirty-worktree checkpointing is in place (commit ff96a5d94) and emits a record_run_health event we can key off of.
Tests can use a fake clock and an in-memory git repo or a synthetic commit-recorder seam — no live system clock or systemd is required.
not_ready_when: The slice changes the watchdog stall threshold, the dead-process detection, or the planner cadence.
The slice silently drops the dirty-worktree checkpoint when a stall is real — the first tick of every distinct stall window must still produce a single observable checkpoint.
acceptance: TestDecideCheckpoint_FirstTickInFreshWindowReturnsFirst: empty state, cfg.Dirty=true, NextWindowID=()=>"w-1" returns (DecisionFirst, {LastCheckpointAt: now, WindowID: "w-1"}).

TestDecideCheckpoint_LaterTickInsideWindowReturnsAmend: prior state {LastCheckpointAt: now-30s, WindowID: "w-1"}, cfg.Dirty=true, windowSeconds=600 returns (DecisionAmend, {LastCheckpointAt: now, WindowID: "w-1"}); NextWindowID is NOT called.

TestDecideCheckpoint_LaterTickPastWindowReturnsFirst: prior state {LastCheckpointAt: now-601s, WindowID: "w-1"}, cfg.Dirty=true, windowSeconds=600, NextWindowID=()=>"w-2" returns (DecisionFirst, {LastCheckpointAt: now, WindowID: "w-2"}).
TestDecideCheckpoint_NoopWhenNotDirty: any prior state, cfg.Dirty=false returns (DecisionNoop, prior state unchanged); NextWindowID is NOT called.
TestDecideCheckpoint_DefaultWindowWhenZeroOrNegative: cfg.WindowSeconds=0 and cfg.WindowSeconds=-5 both behave like windowSeconds=600.
Helper is a pure function with no time.Now, no git invocation, no os.* calls — caller passes both the clock (now) and the dirty flag (cfg.Dirty) and the WindowID generator (cfg.NextWindowID).
items.note: Complete: TDD landed the pure DecideCheckpoint helper and fake-clock fixtures for first checkpoint, in-window amend, post-window rotation, no-dirty noop, and the 600-second fallback for zero/negative window sizes. Pure-Go decider only — shell-script wiring at scripts/orchestrator/watchdog.sh is OUT of scope and will be a separate row that imports DecideCheckpoint via a small stub binary.
done_signal: internal/builderloop/watchdog_coalesce_test.go fixtures prove first/amend/first-after-rotation/noop decisions across a fake clock with no live git or systemd.

provenance.note: Reduces the 74-commit/28-min checkpoint storm observed overnight 2026-04-25 → 2026-04-26 to ≤1 commit per stall window.

items.name: PR-intake idle backoff
items.contract: Pure helper internal/builderloop/pr_intake_backoff.go exposes type BackoffState struct{ConsecutiveEmpty int; SuppressUntil time.Time; Threshold int; Baseline, Idle time.Duration} and methods (s BackoffState) ShouldPoll(now time.Time) bool and (s BackoffState) RecordResult(now time.Time, listed int) BackoffState. After Threshold consecutive listed=0 results, ShouldPoll returns false until now >= SuppressUntil; any listed > 0 result resets ConsecutiveEmpty and clears SuppressUntil. No GitHub API calls, no goroutines, no live clock in this slice.
items.degraded_mode: pr_intake_started/completed events carry an idle_backoff field with the active interval and the consecutive-empty count so dashboards can distinguish "intake was suppressed" from "intake ran and found nothing".
ready_when: pr_intake already emits pr_intake_started and pr_intake_completed ledger events, and pr_intake_completed carries listed=N evidence.
A fake clock and a stub gh-list backend make backoff state observable without live network calls.
not_ready_when: The slice changes how PRs are merged, classified, or evicted — only the schedule of intake runs is in scope.
The slice removes pr_intake calls entirely or makes them event-driven (gh webhook); event-driven is a separate row.
unblocks: Builder-loop self-improvement vs user-feature ratio metric
acceptance: TestBackoffState_ShouldPollWhenNotSuppressed (fresh state, now=t0) returns true.
TestBackoffState_RecordEmptyBelowThreshold accumulates ConsecutiveEmpty without suppressing.
TestBackoffState_RecordEmptyAtThreshold sets SuppressUntil=now+Idle and ShouldPoll=false until SuppressUntil elapses.
TestBackoffState_RecordNonEmptyResetsState restores baseline (ConsecutiveEmpty=0, SuppressUntil zero).
TestBackoffState_ShouldPollAfterSuppressionElapsed flips back to true when now >= SuppressUntil even if ConsecutiveEmpty is still at threshold.
Helper is a pure function — caller injects the clock and the listed result count.
items.note: Pure-Go state machine only. Wiring into pr_intake.go is a separate downstream row. Overnight ledger showed 654 pr_intake_completed events of which only 3 actually merged — this helper is the gate that suppresses 651 idle polls.
done_signal: internal/builderloop/pr_intake_backoff_test.go fixtures prove threshold/suppress/reset/reactivation behavior under a fake clock with no GitHub API and no goroutines.

last_failure.stderr_tail: worker made no changes: 1/1.C/PR-intake idle backoff

provenance.note: Cuts the 99.5% idle-poll rate observed overnight (651/654 polls were listed=0) without changing PR-merge policy.

items.name: Watchdog dead-process vs slow-progress separation
items.contract: TDD packet for a missing pure helper; create exactly two files and do not wire them into the watchdog loop. First add internal/builderloop/watchdog_state_test.go in package builderloop with TestDiagnose table subtests named zero_pid_dead, pid_not_live_after_dead_threshold, live_after_slow_threshold, dead_wins_when_both_thresholds_fire, and healthy_recent_live. Then add internal/builderloop/watchdog_state.go exposing type Verdict string with constants VerdictHealthy="healthy", VerdictSlow="slow", VerdictDead="dead"; type WorkerVitals struct{PID int; LastCommitAt time.Time; PIDIsLive bool}; and Diagnose(now time.Time, v WorkerVitals, deadAfter, slowAfter time.Duration) Verdict. Verdict precedence is: PID==0 => dead, PIDIsLive==false and elapsed>=deadAfter => dead, PIDIsLive==true and elapsed>=slowAfter => slow, else healthy. The helper uses only caller-injected now and PIDIsLive; no os.FindProcess, signal delivery, time.Now, goroutines, watchdog wiring, or config validation.
items.degraded_mode: Watchdog status reports worker_state ∈ {alive_progressing, alive_silent, dead, unknown} and the threshold each one tripped; record_run_health carries the worker_state and which threshold (dead_after_seconds, silent_after_seconds) fired.
ready_when: Existing watchdog timer (commit f96a5d94) emits stall events at a single threshold; this slice carves the threshold into two independent ones.
Watchdog checkpoint coalescing is fixture-ready or validated, so the dead-process tick does not amplify the commit storm.
Both target files are absent on main; the worker's first edit is the focused failing table test, then the helper.
If either target file already exists in the worker checkout, the worker should run the focused test and update this progress row instead of creating a duplicate helper.
not_ready_when: The slice changes how worker output is rejected or how dirty worktrees are committed — only worker liveness detection is in scope.
The slice introduces process-group signal sending or container-aware death detection (those belong to a separate sandboxing row).
The worker needs to edit internal/builderloop/run.go, watchdog timers, backend prompts, ledger writing, or process-kill behavior to make the test pass.
unblocks: Builder-loop self-improvement vs user-feature ratio metric
acceptance: TestDiagnose_HealthyWhenRecentCommitAndAlive: v={PID:1234, LastCommitAt:now-5s, PIDIsLive:true}, deadAfter=120s, slowAfter=600s returns VerdictHealthy.
TestDiagnose_SlowWhenAliveButOverSlowThreshold: v={PID:1234, LastCommitAt:now-700s, PIDIsLive:true} returns VerdictSlow.
TestDiagnose_DeadWhenPIDNotLiveAndOverDeadThreshold: v={PID:1234, LastCommitAt:now-200s, PIDIsLive:false} returns VerdictDead.
TestDiagnose_DeadWhenPIDIsZero: v={PID:0, LastCommitAt:now-1s, PIDIsLive:true} returns VerdictDead (zero PID short-circuits; thresholds and PIDIsLive are ignored).
TestDiagnose_DeadDoesNotDowngradeToSlow: v={PID:1234, LastCommitAt:now-700s, PIDIsLive:false} with deadAfter=120s, slowAfter=600s returns VerdictDead (dead wins over slow when both fire).
TestDiagnose_NotDeadWhenPIDLiveEvenIfSilent: v={PID:1234, LastCommitAt:now-99999s, PIDIsLive:true} returns VerdictSlow (never VerdictDead while the process answers Signal(0)).
Helper is pure — caller injects the clock (now) and the PIDIsLive result.
items.note: Reshape #6 (2026-04-26T18:25:41Z): tightened the no_progress quarantine into a two-file-only helper with named subtests and explicit verdict precedence. The target helper/test files are absent on main; workers should add them directly and must not touch watchdog wiring.
done_signal: internal/builderloop/watchdog_state_test.go fixtures prove healthy/slow/dead verdicts including the dead-vs-slow precedence rule with no os.FindProcess or signal calls.
internal/builderloop/watchdog_state.go contains exactly the new Verdict, WorkerVitals, and Diagnose helper API; no existing builderloop runtime file changes are required.

items.name: Builder-loop self-improvement vs user-feature ratio metric
items.contract: TDD packet for a missing pure helper; create exactly two files and do not parse the live ledger. First add internal/builderloop/ship_ratio_test.go in package builderloop with two tests: TestClassifySubphase covers exact "1.C", slash-prefixed "1/1.C", exact "5.O", slash-prefixed "5/5.O", prefix "control-plane/", exact/prefix "4.", "6.", "7.", blanks, and unknown values; TestComputeShipRatio covers inclusive window start, future-event exclusion, per-kind counts, and Total. Then add internal/builderloop/ship_ratio.go exposing type RowKind string with constants RowKindSelfImprovement="self_improvement", RowKindUserFeature="user_feature", RowKindUnclassified="unclassified"; type ShippedRowEvent struct{SubphaseID string; ShippedAt time.Time}; type ShipRatio struct{SelfImprovement int; UserFeature int; Unclassified int; Total int}; ClassifySubphase(subphaseID string) RowKind; and ComputeShipRatio(events []ShippedRowEvent, window time.Duration, now time.Time) ShipRatio. ClassifySubphase trims spaces and accepts both bare subphase IDs and phase/subphase paths. No file I/O, ledger parsing, record_run_health emission, goroutines, or live clock.
items.degraded_mode: When the ship_ratio cannot be computed (insufficient history, classification ambiguous), record_run_health carries ship_ratio=null and reports ship_ratio_evidence with the reason instead of fabricating zero.
ready_when: record_run_health is the canonical health signal already (commit 2653a7b6 etc.) and runs.jsonl carries shipped row evidence.
A subphase classifier mapping (1.C, control-plane/*, and 5.O → self_improvement; 4.*, 6.*, and 7.* → user_feature; blank/unknown → unclassified) can live in a small in-package table for now.
internal/builderloop/ship_ratio.go and ship_ratio_test.go are absent on main; the worker's first edit is the focused failing test file.
The dependency on PR-intake idle backoff is already satisfied on main; only Watchdog dead-process vs slow-progress separation remains a live prerequisite.
not_ready_when: The slice changes ship-detection or ledger-write semantics — only adds a derived counter.
The slice depends on a yet-unbuilt classifier service or external store.
The worker needs to read .codex/builder-loop/state/runs.jsonl or change record_run_health to make the tests pass.
acceptance: TestClassifySubphase_KnownSelfImprovement covers '1.C', '1/1.C/Watchdog dead-process vs slow-progress separation', 'control-plane/backend', and '5/5.O/CLI log snapshot reader' returning RowKindSelfImprovement.
TestClassifySubphase_KnownUserFeature covers '4.A', '4.H', '6.A', '7.B' returning RowKindUserFeature.
TestClassifySubphase_UnknownReturnsUnclassified (e.g., '99.X') returns RowKindUnclassified.
TestComputeShipRatio_FiltersByWindow excludes events older than now-window.
TestComputeShipRatio_CountsAllKinds returns SelfImprovement, UserFeature, Unclassified counts and a Total.

items.name: Slack CommandRegistry parser wiring
items.contract: Slack message and slash-command ingress routes through the shared gateway CommandRegistry instead of maintaining adapter-local slash parsing
items.degraded_mode: Unknown Slack slash commands return the shared gateway unknown-command behavior while recognized help/new/stop commands keep Slack-specific ack and thread reply semantics.
ready_when: Slack Socket Mode adapter and thread/coalescing tests are already complete, and gateway.ParseInboundText plus SlackSubcommandMap are available locally.
not_ready_when: The slice rewrites Slack onto gateway.Manager, changes Slack config loading, or registers Slack in cmd/gormes gateway instead of only proving shared parser usage.
acceptance: Slack message events call gateway.ParseInboundText for /help, /new, /stop, unknown slash commands, and plain text submits.
Slack slash-command envelopes are retained for bot-level Ack and forwarded as the slash text consumed by the shared parser.
Slack help output is rendered from gateway.GatewayHelpLines so registry changes affect Telegram, Slack, and gateway help consistently.
items.note: Implementation already landed before this planner pass: internal/slack.Bot.handleEvent calls gateway.ParseInboundText, slackHelpText renders gateway.GatewayHelpLines, internal/slack/real_client.go forwards Slack slash commands as shared parser text, and internal/slack/bot_test.go covers registry-driven message commands plus slash-command forwarding.

Run translates Slack Event values into gateway.InboundEvent with platform=slack, chat_id=channel_id, optional thread_id, user_id, message_id/timestamp, and normalized text kind from gateway.ParseInboundText.
Send posts plain text to the correct Slack channel/thread and returns the Slack timestamp as msgID without opening a real Slack connection in tests.
Existing internal/slack bot/coalescing tests stay green, proving the shim did not rewrite the current standalone behavior.

provenance.note: Config and cmd/gormes registration are Gormes-owned public surfaces built on the local shared gateway chassis.

items.name: Bridge-vs-native runtime decision
items.note: TDD landed: internal/channels/whatsapp.DecideRuntime ports the upstream `gateway/platforms/whatsapp.py` runtime-selection seam into a Go contract that freezes bridge-first versus native-first startup, Gormes state-root/session-path ownership, bridge command/log shape, and bot versus self-chat echo/prefix policy before transport wiring.

items.name: WhatsApp identity resolution + self-chat guard
items.contract: WhatsApp bot identity, self-chat suppression, and peer mapping stay deterministic across bridge and native runtimes
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status reports unresolved WhatsApp bot identity instead of accepting self-chat loops or ambiguous peer mappings.
ready_when: The bridge-vs-native runtime decision identifies which identity source owns the bot/self peer for a session.
not_ready_when: Identity rules are hidden inside send/reconnect code instead of fixture-tested before transport wiring.
acceptance: Bridge and native identity inputs produce stable gateway peer IDs.
Messages from the bot's own identity are ignored or surfaced as self-chat suppression, not routed back into the kernel.
Group and DM peers preserve raw platform IDs needed for outbound delivery.
items.note: TDD landed: internal/channels/whatsapp now exposes the runtime-owned bot identity source, fixture-tested bridge/native peer canonicalization, raw WhatsApp reply peers for outbound delivery, self-chat suppression decisions, and unresolved bot identity degraded status before pairing/reconnect/send wiring.
done_signal: WhatsApp identity fixtures prove bridge/native peer IDs and self-chat suppression before transport sends land.

items.name: Inbound normalization + command passthrough
items.note: TDD landed: internal/channels/whatsapp.NormalizeInbound normalizes bridge/native peer IDs and routes generic slash commands through gateway.ParseInboundText.

items.name: Pairing, reconnect, and send contract
items.note: Closeout: this umbrella was split into `WhatsApp outbound pairing gate + raw peer mapping` and `WhatsApp reconnect backoff + send retry policy`, and both split rows are validated complete with internal/channels/whatsapp send and reconnect fixtures. Do not reuse this row for future runtime work; add a new small row for any remaining WhatsApp drift.

items.name: WhatsApp outbound pairing gate + raw peer mapping
items.contract: WhatsApp outbound sends are gated by pairing state and map normalized gateway chat IDs back to bridge/native raw peers
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status reports unpaired or unresolved WhatsApp targets before attempting a transport send.
ready_when: Bridge/native runtime selection, bot identity, self-chat suppression, and inbound normalization are already fixture-locked.
not_ready_when: The slice adds reconnect loops, process supervision, or native transport startup instead of only freezing send gating and raw peer mapping.
unblocks: WhatsApp reconnect backoff + send retry policy
acceptance: Unpaired outbound targets return a visible degraded result and do not call the send transport.
Bridge and native raw DM/group peer IDs are reconstructed from the normalized gateway chat/session handle.
Reply metadata from the originating inbound event is preserved in the outbound send request.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed a transport-neutral WhatsApp gateway channel send gate. internal/channels/whatsapp/send_contract_test.go proves unpaired and unresolved outbound targets return degraded status without touching the fake sender, while paired bridge DM and native group sends reconstruct raw WhatsApp peers from normalized gateway chat IDs and preserve inbound reply message metadata.
done_signal: WhatsApp send fixtures prove unpaired targets are blocked and paired raw peers preserve DM/group plus reply metadata.

items.name: WhatsApp reconnect backoff + send retry policy
items.contract: WhatsApp send failures use bounded reconnect/backoff semantics without duplicating terminal replies or hiding unresolved runtime state
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status exposes reconnect pending/exhausted state and the original send target when retries cannot recover.
ready_when: Outbound pairing gates and raw peer mapping are fixture-locked and transport senders can be faked.
not_ready_when: The slice introduces real bridge/native process startup, QR pairing UX, or long-running sleeps in tests.
blocked_by: WhatsApp outbound pairing gate + raw peer mapping
acceptance: Retry policy uses deterministic bounded backoff under a fake clock.
Reconnect exhaustion emits one visible terminal/degraded result, not duplicate sends.
Successful reconnect retries preserve the original raw peer and reply metadata.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/channels/whatsapp/reconnect_contract_test.go with a fake-clock retry policy over the transport-neutral WhatsApp Bot. Fixtures prove bounded reconnect backoff, reconnect-pending/exhausted outbound status with the original normalized/raw target and send error, preservation of raw peer plus reply metadata across successful retries, and no synthetic duplicate terminal send on exhaustion.
done_signal: WhatsApp reconnect fixtures prove bounded retry, retry exhaustion degradation, and no duplicate terminal sends.

items.name: Gateway session store + SessionSource parity
items.note: TDD landed: gateway session-ID resolution now prefers stored handles, falls back deterministically to the chat-scoped key on empty/error paths, and freezes SessionSource/session-context prompt contracts in gateway tests.

items.name: SessionContext prompt injection
items.note: TDD landed: each inbound submit now injects a stable Current Session Context system block ahead of recall/user messages, covering source metadata plus deterministic delivery-target exposure.

items.name: BlueBubbles iMessage session-context prompt guidance
items.contract: Gateway session-context prompts tell the agent when the origin is BlueBubbles/iMessage and ask for short, blank-line-separated message bubbles
items.degraded_mode: Until this lands, BlueBubbles still sends replies through the first-pass adapter but the model is not explicitly guided toward iMessage-length bubble formatting.
ready_when: BlueBubbles outbound formatting splits blank-line paragraphs into separate iMessage sends, so prompt guidance has a matching delivery contract.
not_ready_when: The slice changes general session-context ordering or adds provider/runtime behavior instead of only adding the platform-specific BlueBubbles note.
acceptance: BuildSessionContextPrompt includes an iMessage/BlueBubbles platform note for source platform `bluebubbles`.
The note asks for short conversational replies and blank-line-separated blocks that map to separate bubbles.
Non-BlueBubbles session-context prompts remain byte-for-byte stable except for intentional test fixture updates.
items.note: TDD: mirror upstream f731c2c2 `gateway/session.py` prompt guidance in Gormes' `internal/gateway/session_context.go`, but keep it blocked until the BlueBubbles send formatter can actually honor blank-line bubble boundaries.
done_signal: Session-context tests prove BlueBubbles origins receive iMessage bubble guidance while other platform prompts stay stable.

items.name: DeliveryRouter + --deliver target parsing
items.note: TDD landed: --deliver parsing now covers origin/local/platform/platform:chat/platform:chat:thread forms with explicit invalid-shape tests before future adapter routing binds them to live channels.

items.name: Gateway stream consumer for agent-event fan-out
items.note: TDD landed: a typed GatewayStreamConsumer contract now replays one render frame to multiple delivery targets in deterministic order and preserves later deliveries when one sink fails.

items.name: Non-editable gateway progress/commentary send fallback
items.contract: Channels without placeholder/edit capabilities receive progress-safe interim or final assistant messages through the plain Send path without EditMessage calls
items.degraded_mode: Non-editable channels continue to receive final responses, but quick commentary/interim updates may be suppressed until the send-fallback fixture proves no edit path is attempted.
ready_when: A fake channel can implement only gateway.Channel while the manager render path is exercised from an originating inbound event.
not_ready_when: The slice adds platform-specific BlueBubbles logic to gateway.Manager instead of testing capability-based behavior shared by all non-editable channels.
acceptance: A Channel-only fake receives terminal assistant output through Send with the original chat target.
The same fixture proves no SendPlaceholder or EditMessage method is required for non-editable channels.
Editable channel streaming/coalescing tests keep their existing placeholder/edit behavior.
items.note: TDD landed: manager outbound tests now exercise a Channel-only fake from an originating inbound event, proving interim/commentary and terminal assistant output use the plain Send path with the original chat target while editable channels keep placeholder/edit coalescing.
done_signal: Gateway manager tests cover both editable streaming and Channel-only send fallback without platform-specific branches.

items.name: WeCom + WeiXin shared-chassis bot seam
items.note: TDD landed: internal/channels/wecom and internal/channels/weixin now pin policy-gated ingress, request/reply routing, and per-platform reply-path contracts before any SDK wiring.

items.name: WeCom + WeiXin transport/bootstrap layer
items.note: TDD landed: internal/channels/wecom/runtime.go and internal/channels/weixin/runtime.go now freeze WeCom WebSocket/callback bootstrap, credential validation, WeCom reply-vs-active-push decisions, and Weixin long-poll/context-token lifecycle seams before any real transport binding.

items.name: Discord forum channel ingress + thread lifecycle
items.note: TDD landed: Discord forum channel type detection, parent-forum chat IDs with canonical thread IDs for forum post ingress, and open/closed/archived thread lifecycle normalization now emit fixture-backed gateway events without changing the shipped 2.B.2 send contract or shared chassis behavior.

items.name: Discord SessionSource guild/parent/message evidence
items.contract: Discord ingress preserves guild, parent channel, and triggering message IDs in the gateway SessionSource without changing existing chat/thread routing
items.degraded_mode: Gateway session context omits unavailable Discord scope IDs explicitly rather than fabricating guild/thread/message metadata.
ready_when: Discord forum ingress and thread lifecycle fixtures are green on top of the shared gateway InboundEvent envelope.
not_ready_when: The slice changes Discord send behavior, tool schemas, or forum media handling instead of only preserving source metadata through fixtures.
blocked_by: Discord forum channel ingress + thread lifecycle
acceptance: Inbound Discord events can carry guild_id, parent_chat_id, and message_id through InboundEvent into SessionSource and SessionContext prompt rendering.
Forum/thread messages preserve the parent channel/forum ID separately from the effective ChatID and canonical ThreadID.
DMs and platforms without these identifiers render no fabricated values and preserve existing chat/thread routing.
items.note: TDD landed: fixture-backed Discord ingress now preserves guild_id, parent_chat_id, and message_id as gateway SessionSource metadata, renders only available values in session context, and keeps existing forum ChatID/ThreadID routing unchanged.
done_signal: Discord session-source metadata fixtures prove guild, parent-channel, and triggering-message IDs flow into session context without route regressions.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the new Discord source metadata contract; Gormes keeps the typed gateway envelope and shared session-context rendering.

items.name: Discord forum media + polish parity
ready_when: Discord forum ingress and thread lifecycle fixtures are green on top of the shipped Discord adapter
blocked_by: Discord forum channel ingress + thread lifecycle
items.note: TDD: port upstream PR #607be54a (forum channel media + polish) after the ingress slice is green — attachment flow for forum posts, initial-post vs reply differences, and deterministic outbound routing to forum threads. Keep the shared-chassis send contract intact so non-forum Discord behavior cannot regress.

items.name: Architecture planner tasks manager script
items.note: TDD landed for the main tracked entrypoint: scripts/gormes-architecture-planner-tasks-manager.sh writes .codex/planner/architecture-planner-tasks.md plus report/state artifacts, logs verbose repo/upstream/progress/validation/schedule context, and installs a periodic systemd/cron run. Phase 1.C later restored the compatibility wrappers at scripts/gormes-architecture-task-manager.sh and scripts/architectureplanneragent.sh.

2.E.1.name: OS-AI Spine: Delegation Policy + Child Execution

items.name: Runner-enforced tool allowlists + blocked-tool policy
items.note: TDD landed: blocked-tool and allowlist tests now cover the runner execution seam before any child tool call reaches the executor.

items.name: Tool-call audit in typed child results
items.note: TDD landed: manager/result and delegate_task output tests now require typed child tool-call events to survive into terminal result envelopes.

items.name: Real child Hermes stream loop
items.note: TDD landed: HermesRunner now executes a real child OpenStream loop with token/output streaming, multi-iteration tool_calls continuation through executeChildTool, explicit stream-open error handling, and registry wiring that opts delegate_task into the real runner whenever a live Hermes client is available.

items.name: GBrain minion-orchestrator routing policy
items.contract: Durable-job routing separates deterministic restart-survivable work from live LLM subagents, following GBrain's unified minion-orchestrator skill while keeping Gormes Go-native subagent APIs
items.degraded_mode: Until the policy lands, Gormes exposes in-memory subagents plus append-only run logs only; durable minion routing is documented as unavailable in status/doctor surfaces.
ready_when: Phase 2.E.0 and 2.E.1 subagent runtime slices are complete and append-only run logs exist for child executions.
not_ready_when: The slice ports GBrain's Postgres/PGLite queue, shell-job executor, pause/resume runtime, or supervisor instead of only fixture-locking routing decisions and trust boundaries.
acceptance: Policy fixtures classify deterministic shell/cron-like work separately from judgment-heavy LLM subagents.
Child-agent trust cannot submit privileged deterministic shell jobs through the durable-job route.
Existing delegate_task and subagent manager APIs remain Go-native and are not renamed to Minions.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed a pure Gormes routing policy fixture in internal/subagent/minion_policy_test.go. Deterministic shell/cron-like work routes to a privileged durable-job lane for operator/system trust, judgment-heavy LLM work stays on the live Go-native delegate_task subagent lane, child-agent trust is denied for deterministic durable submits, and no GBrain queue/executor/supervisor runtime was imported.
done_signal: Routing policy tests prove which work enters durable orchestration, which trust classes may submit it, and that existing delegate_task names remain stable.

items.name: Durable subagent/job ledger
items.contract: SQLite-first job ledger records restartable subagent and deterministic work state with claim, progress, result, error, parent-child, and cancellation fields
items.degraded_mode: Doctor and status report append-only run logs without restart rehydration until the durable ledger can claim and resume work.
ready_when: Routing policy fixtures define which work may enter durable orchestration and which callers are allowed to submit or observe each lane.
not_ready_when: The slice tries to implement every GBrain Minions status, Postgres/PGLite compatibility, supervisor process management, or arbitrary shell-job submission.
acceptance: A SQLite-backed ledger records job id, job kind, status, parent id, depth, progress JSON, result JSON, error text, timestamps, and cancellation intent.
Subagent and cron/deterministic job fixtures use the same ledger contract without changing existing public delegate_task behavior.
Status or doctor output distinguishes append-only run logs from durable restart/replay support.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed a SQLite-first durable ledger in internal/subagent with submit, claim, claim-by-id, expired-lock reclaim, renew, progress, complete, fail, cancel intent, parent child_done events, and restart/replay status. Cron executor and subagent manager can opt into the same ledger without changing append-only run logs or delegate_task output, and internal/doctor distinguishes append-only logs from durable restart/replay availability.
done_signal: Durable ledger tests prove minimal restartable job state exists and doctor/status surfaces expose whether replay is available.

items.name: Interrupt propagation to concurrent-tool workers
items.note: TDD landed: executeToolCalls now fans tool_calls out through a shared cancellable context while the kernel continues servicing cancel/reset/submit events, returns the coherent {"error":"tool execution cancelled"} envelope for cancelled workers, and stops the turn without a follow-up model call. Fixture tests cover one interrupt cancelling every in-flight worker, execute_code sidecar cancellation, delegated subagent child cancellation, and LocalCodeSandbox interrupted status.

items.name: Durable job backpressure + timeout audit
items.contract: Gormes durable jobs expose GBrain-style max-waiting backpressure, wall-clock timeout evidence, and operator-readable queue health without importing Minions runtime code
items.degraded_mode: Doctor/status reports queue full, timeout-at, and stale waiting counts before accepting more durable work.
ready_when: The SQLite-first durable subagent/job ledger is complete and records claim, progress, completion, failure, and cancellation intent.
not_ready_when: The slice ports GBrain's PGLite/Postgres queue, CLI worker loop, or shell handler instead of adding bounded queue admission and audit fields to the existing Go ledger.
acceptance: Submit fixtures reject or degrade when waiting durable jobs exceed a configured maxWaiting limit.
Wall-clock timeout_at is recorded and surfaced separately from context cancellation.
Doctor/status evidence reports waiting, claimed, timed-out, and backpressure-denied counts.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/subagent/durable_backpressure_test.go over the Go SQLite ledger. Durable submissions now reject with audited backpressure evidence when max_waiting is reached, claims persist timeout_at wall-clock evidence separately from cancellation intent, and doctor/status surfaces waiting, claimed, timed-out, stale waiting, queue-full, and backpressure-denied counts without importing GBrain Minions runtime code.
done_signal: Durable backpressure fixtures prove max-waiting rejection, timeout_at audit fields, and doctor/status queue health over the Go ledger.

items.name: Durable worker supervisor status seam
items.contract: Gormes reports durable-worker liveness, restart intent, and degraded/no-worker modes before any external supervisor controls job execution
items.degraded_mode: Doctor/status reports no durable worker, stale heartbeat, or supervisor-unavailable instead of silently accepting restart-survivable work.
ready_when: Backpressure and timeout audit fields are visible through the durable ledger status surface.
not_ready_when: The slice starts subprocess workers, writes systemd units, or ports GBrain's supervisor loop; it only freezes the status contract Gormes must expose first.
acceptance: Status fixtures distinguish no-worker, healthy heartbeat, stale heartbeat, and supervisor-unavailable states.
Restart intent is represented as data/audit evidence, not an implicit process launch.
Existing delegate_task and live child-agent APIs remain Go-native and are not renamed to Minions.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/subagent/durable_supervisor_status_test.go over the Go SQLite ledger. Durable status now distinguishes no-worker, healthy heartbeat, stale heartbeat, and supervisor-unavailable states, records restart intent as audit data, and doctor output surfaces worker liveness, supervisor availability, and restart-intent evidence without starting worker processes or renaming Go-native delegate_task APIs to Minions.
done_signal: Durable supervisor status fixtures prove worker liveness/degraded modes without starting external worker processes.

items.name: Durable pause/resume intent contract
items.contract: Gormes durable jobs record pause and resume as explicit operator/system control intents over the SQLite ledger without starting a GBrain worker daemon
items.degraded_mode: Doctor/status reports paused jobs, resume intent, and unsupported child-agent lifecycle control instead of silently leaving workers active.
ready_when: Durable worker supervisor status is complete and the SQLite ledger can already report worker liveness, cancellation intent, and replay availability.
not_ready_when: The slice starts external workers, implements shell execution, or ports GBrain Postgres/PGLite queue internals instead of only freezing pause/resume state transitions.
unblocks: Durable replay and inbox message contract
acceptance: Pause transitions waiting or active durable jobs into an observable paused state with actor, reason, and timestamp evidence.
Resume returns paused jobs to the correct claimable state without losing progress_json, parent_id, timeout_at, or lock audit fields.
Child-agent trust cannot pause or resume privileged deterministic shell/cron jobs.
Doctor/status includes paused and resume-pending counts plus degraded evidence when lifecycle control is unsupported.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/subagent/durable_lifecycle_test.go over the Go SQLite ledger. Durable jobs now record pause/resume as explicit operator/system lifecycle intents, transition waiting or active work through a paused state with actor/reason/timestamp evidence, resume paused work back to a claimable waiting state without losing progress, parent, timeout, or lock audit fields, deny child-agent lifecycle control for privileged deterministic jobs, and surface paused, resume-pending, and unsupported lifecycle-control counts through doctor/status without starting worker daemons.
done_signal: Durable lifecycle fixtures prove pause/resume state transitions, trust denial, and doctor/status counts over the Go SQLite ledger.

provenance.note: GBrain defines the minion lifecycle semantics; Gormes adapts them to its Go-native durable ledger without importing the Minions runtime.

items.name: Durable replay and inbox message contract
items.contract: Gormes durable jobs can replay completed/failed work and store steer messages as auditable inbox data without exposing protected shell submission to child agents
items.degraded_mode: Status reports replay unavailable, inbox-unread, and protected-submit denial separately so operators can distinguish observation from mutation.
ready_when: Pause/resume intent fixtures are landed and durable ledger status exposes lifecycle-control state without requiring an external worker.
not_ready_when: The slice implements arbitrary shell execution, a full CLI, token accounting, or fan-out aggregators before replay/inbox storage and trust boundaries are fixture-locked.
acceptance: Replay creates a new job linked to replay_of with data_overrides preserved as JSON and without mutating the original terminal job.
Inbox messages store sender, directive payload, unread/read timestamps, and are only claimable by the owning job or operator/system trust.
Child-agent trust may observe get/list/progress-style durable job state but cannot submit protected shell_command work.
PGLite or in-process fallback is documented as a Gormes-local mode, not as a requirement to port GBrain's database engine.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/subagent/durable_replay_inbox_test.go over the Go SQLite ledger. Replay creates a fresh waiting job linked by replay_of, preserves data_overrides_json, and leaves the terminal source job untouched. Durable inbox messages persist sender/trust, directive payload, unread/read timestamps, and owner/operator/system claim boundaries. Child-agent trust can observe get/list/progress-style durable state while protected shell_command submit is denied and audited separately. Doctor/status now reports replay-unavailable, inbox-unread, and protected-submit-denied evidence independently. Gormes keeps this as a local SQLite/in-process durable ledger mode rather than porting GBrain's PGLite/Postgres engine or shell worker.
done_signal: Replay/inbox fixtures prove replay lineage, steer-message audit, and protected shell-submit denial without importing GBrain Minions.

provenance.note: Upstream provides the lifecycle and protection model; Gormes owns the Go-native ledger representation.

drift_state.origin_decision: GBrain 11abb24 unified the jobs lane into minion-orchestrator. Gormes now has Go-native durable routing, ledger, backpressure, supervisor status, pause/resume, replay, inbox, and protected-submit denial slices validated over SQLite; future GBrain drift should become new small rows instead of keeping this subphase porting.

items.name: Canonical CommandDef registry
items.note: TDD landed: internal/gateway now owns one CommandRegistry with ResolveCommand, ParseInboundText, registry-derived help output, and canonical alias handling for /start -> /help.

items.name: Gateway slash dispatch + per-platform exposure
items.note: TDD landed: Telegram and Discord parse through the shared gateway command layer, the manager welcome/help text is registry-driven, and deterministic Telegram/Slack exposure helpers now derive from the same source of truth.

items.name: Gateway per-event hook registry
items.note: TDD landed: manager lifecycle tests now lock before/after receive, direct and placeholder-backed before/after send, and on_error hook emission on the shared gateway boundary.

items.name: Hook manifest discovery + handler loading
items.note: TDD landed: HOOK.yaml directories now load executable command handlers with exact, prefix-wildcard, and global event matching, and `gormes gateway` wires the XDG hooks root into the live manager.

items.name: Built-in BOOT.md startup hook
items.note: TDD landed: gateway startup now reads $XDG_DATA_HOME/gormes/BOOT.md, runs it through an isolated background boot kernel, and treats missing files or boot execution failures as non-blocking startup conditions.

items.name: Graceful restart drain + managed shutdown
items.note: TDD landed: manager shutdown tests now lock drain-until-idle, shutdown timeout, submit rejection during shutdown, and signal-driven root-context cancellation only after the drain path returns.

items.name: Adapter startup failure cleanup contract
items.note: TDD landed: gateway Manager.Run now treats channel startup Run errors as startup failures, defensively calls Disconnect on partially-started adapters, logs cleanup errors without masking the original failure, and Discord startup-open failures close their session through that contract.

items.name: Active-turn follow-up queue + late-arrival drain policy
items.note: TDD landed: the shared gateway manager now queues ordinary EventSubmit follow-ups behind the active turn, preserves original terminal-frame routing, drains queued follow-ups only after terminal frames, and keeps the active-turn guard pinned while draining so late arrivals queue behind the drained turn instead of duplicating kernel submits. Tests cover cross-channel route preservation and late-arrival FIFO behavior.

items.name: Drain-timeout resume_pending recovery
items.contract: Gateway restart/shutdown drain timeouts preserve resumable session identity and inject a reason-aware resume note on the next turn without overriding hard-stuck or suspended state
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status reports resume_pending, drain_timeout, and non-resumable stuck/suspended evidence instead of silently dropping in-flight session context.
ready_when: Graceful restart drain + managed shutdown and Active-turn follow-up queue + late-arrival drain policy are complete on main.
The slice can be tested with a fake manager/session read model and a fake clock; no live Telegram, Discord, Slack, or provider process is required.
not_ready_when: The slice changes pairing approval, gateway status CLI rendering, token locks, or service-manager restart behavior instead of only freezing drain-timeout resume metadata.
acceptance: A drain timeout marks only sessions whose agent turn is still running at timeout as resume_pending with session_id, source, drain reason, and timestamp.
The next EventSubmit for the same session prepends one reason-aware resume note and clears resume_pending after the handoff is accepted.
Hard suspended, cancelled, or stuck-loop state takes precedence over resume_pending and emits explicit non-resumable evidence.
Late-arrival queued follow-ups keep FIFO ordering and do not create a second resume note.
items.note: TDD landed: drain-timeout resume_pending fixtures preserve the running session identity with source/reason/timestamp evidence, inject exactly one restart/shutdown-aware resume note on the next accepted turn, clear the marker after handoff, preserve queued follow-up FIFO order, and let suspended/cancelled/stuck-loop non-resumable evidence override resume recovery without touching restart service control, PID validation, token locks, or status CLI rendering.
done_signal: Resume-pending fixtures prove drain-timeout sessions retain identity, inject exactly one restart note, and lose to hard non-resumable states.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the restart/drain recovery behavior; Gormes adapts it to the shared Go gateway manager and session read model.

items.name: Pairing read-model schema + atomic persistence
items.contract: Gateway pairing state is persisted as a Go-native XDG read model with atomic writes, deterministic pending/approved ordering, and per-platform paired/unpaired status before approval UX
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status reports missing, corrupt, or permission-denied pairing state without starting transports or accepting unknown users.
ready_when: The shared gateway package already owns RuntimeStatusStore and can add a pairing read model without live adapter startup.
No approval-code generation or unauthorized-DM response behavior is needed for this first persistence slice.
not_ready_when: The implementation wires Telegram/Discord/Slack pairing UX, generates codes, or starts channel transports instead of only freezing the persisted read model.
acceptance: Pairing store fixtures create pending and approved user records per platform under an XDG-controlled state root.
Writes use temp-file plus atomic rename, preserve valid old state on failed writes, and apply owner-only file mode where the platform supports chmod.
Corrupt JSON and missing files return structured degraded evidence and deterministic empty read models.
List output is sorted by platform, user_id, and code age so docs/status tests do not depend on map iteration order.
items.note: TDD landed: internal/gateway.PairingStore now persists a Go-owned XDG pairing.json read model with explicit pending and approved per-platform records, temp-file plus atomic rename writes, owner-only file mode, structured missing/corrupt/permission-denied degraded reads, deterministic platform/user/code-age ordering, and no code-generation or adapter UX wiring.
done_signal: Pairing store fixtures prove atomic persistence, deterministic readout, corrupt-state degradation, and per-platform pending/approved separation without code generation or adapter UX.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the operator-visible pairing persistence contract; Gormes owns the Go/XDG read model and status evidence.

items.name: Pairing approval + rate-limit semantics
items.contract: Pairing approval uses Hermes-compatible code generation, expiry, pending limits, failed-approval lockout, per-user rate limits, and Telegram DM from_user fallback without starting agent sessions
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status distinguishes rate-limited, max-pending, expired, locked-out, allowlist-denied, and unresolved-user pairing attempts.
ready_when: Pairing read-model schema + atomic persistence is complete and exposes fake-clock-friendly pending/approved state.
Telegram DM events can be represented through gateway.InboundEvent or a tiny gateway-owned resolver fixture without importing python-telegram-bot.
not_ready_when: The slice sends platform replies, implements unauthorized-DM mode selection, edits adapter code, or adds live Telegram SDK dependencies.
acceptance: Generated codes use an 8-character unambiguous alphabet, cryptographic randomness, one-hour expiry, and max three pending codes per platform.
Failed approval attempts lock a platform after five invalid codes for one hour while valid codes approve and remove the pending record.
Per-user request rate limiting blocks repeat code requests for ten minutes and records visible rate-limit evidence.

Telegram private-chat events with from_user missing resolve user_id from chat.id; non-DM events with from_user missing do not synthesize a user_id; allowlist-denied users still hit the failed-attempt/lockout evidence path.
items.note: TDD landed: internal/gateway.PairingStore now owns Hermes-compatible pairing approval policy over the Go read model: cryptographic 8-character unambiguous codes, one-hour expiry, max three pending codes per platform, ten-minute per-user request rate limits with status evidence, five-failure one-hour platform lockout, valid-code approval that removes pending records, Telegram private-chat missing-from_user fallback through InboundEvent.ChatType, and typed evidence for rate-limited, max-pending, expired, locked-out, allowlist-denied, and unresolved-user attempts without adapter sends or agent sessions.
done_signal: Pairing approval fixtures prove code entropy/expiry, pending limits, lockout, per-user rate limiting, and Telegram from_user fallback without adapter sends or agent sessions.

provenance.note: Hermes defines pairing security semantics; Gormes adapts them to gateway-owned Go fixtures and typed degraded evidence.

items.name: Unauthorized DM pairing response contract
items.contract: Unknown direct-message users receive the configured deny, pair, or ignore response without leaking authorized-session state
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status and logs show denied or pending-pair users without starting agent sessions.
ready_when: Pairing approval, rate limiting, allowlist checks, and Telegram missing-from_user fallback are fixture-locked in internal/gateway/pairing_approval_test.go.
not_ready_when: The slice changes code generation, reads live platform SDKs, or starts agent sessions for unknown direct-message users.
acceptance: Configured deny mode sends a deterministic denial without creating a session.
Configured pair mode sends one bounded pairing prompt and records pending state.
Configured ignore mode produces no platform reply and no agent run.
Group/channel unauthorized messages stay silent unless a future platform-specific row explicitly opts into group pairing.
items.note: TDD landed: internal/gateway.HandleUnauthorizedDM now freezes the shared unknown-DM policy over fake channels and PairingStore: deny mode sends a deterministic public denial and records allowlist-denied evidence, pair mode emits one bounded approval prompt while pending/rate-limited state stays in pairing.json, ignore mode stays silent, and group/channel unauthorized messages do not reply or start agent sessions.
done_signal: Unauthorized-DM fixtures prove deny, pair, and ignore modes never start an agent session for unknown users.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the deny/pair/ignore public behavior; Gormes keeps it in shared gateway policy before adapter-specific UX.

items.name: `gormes gateway status` read-only command
items.contract: A read-only `gormes gateway status` command renders configured channels, pairing state, and runtime lifecycle from stores without starting transports or agent sessions
items.degraded_mode: The command reports no-channel, missing/corrupt pairing state, missing runtime state, and failed channel lifecycle evidence without opening Telegram, Discord, Slack, memory, or provider clients.
ready_when: Pairing read-model schema + atomic persistence, Pairing approval + rate-limit semantics, Unauthorized DM pairing response contract, and Channel lifecycle writers into status model are complete on main.
No `cmd/gormes/gateway_status.go` or `internal/gateway/statusview.go` exists on main yet, so this slice must start with failing command/render tests.
not_ready_when: The slice starts gateway transports, opens the memory store, performs provider calls, changes pairing approval policy, or implements PID/process validation in the same change.
acceptance: A pure renderer over configured channels, PairingStatus, and RuntimeStatus emits deterministic channel ordering and stable paired/unpaired/lifecycle text.
`gormes gateway status` succeeds with no configured channels by returning a visible no-channel status instead of the runtime command's startup error.
Configured Telegram/Discord/Slack rows can render running, stopped, failed, paired, pending, approved, and degraded pairing evidence from fake stores.
Focused command tests prove status execution does not construct real channel clients, session maps, memory stores, providers, or gateway.Manager.
items.note: TDD landed: cmd/gormes gateway status now renders configured gateway channels through a pure internal/gateway status view over PairingStatus and RuntimeStatus snapshots, reports no-channel and missing runtime/pairing evidence without falling through to gateway startup, and command tests prove bogus Telegram/Discord credentials do not construct transports, session maps, memory stores, providers, or gateway.Manager.
done_signal: Gateway status command and pure renderer tests prove configured channel, pairing, and runtime status can be inspected without starting transports.

provenance.note: The command is a Gormes-owned operator surface over Go-native pairing/runtime stores; upstream gateway/status.py only informs the read-model shape.

items.name: Runtime status JSON + PID/process validation
items.contract: Gateway runtime status validates PID/start-time identity and stale-process cleanup before callers trust gateway_state.json as the live lifecycle read model
items.degraded_mode: Runtime status distinguishes missing state, stale PID, pid-reused mismatch, stopped process, and permission-denied process checks instead of claiming a gateway is live from old JSON.
ready_when: `gormes gateway status` read-only command has established the operator-facing status view and can consume PID validation evidence.
The slice can use a fake process table and temp gateway_state.json/PID paths; no live system process control is required.
not_ready_when: The slice invokes real kill/systemctl/sc.exe, adds token-scoped locks, changes channel lifecycle write semantics, or edits pairing approval behavior.
acceptance: RuntimeStatusStore writes PID, start_time, generation, and command identity fields atomically with lifecycle updates.
Read/validation fixtures classify missing PID file, stale stopped PID, reused PID with mismatched start time, permission-denied process lookup, and live matching process.
Stale state cleanup preserves last_error evidence while refusing to mark channels running.
The status renderer can display the validation result without starting or stopping any service.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed RuntimeStatusStore PID identity records with start_time, generation, command, and argv fields in gateway_state.json plus the sibling gateway.pid record. Fake process-table fixtures prove missing state, missing PID file, stale PID, PID reuse, stopped process, permission-denied lookup, and live matching process classification; stale cleanup returns stopped channel state without dropping exit/error evidence, and `gormes gateway status` renders runtime_validation evidence from the read-only status path.
done_signal: PID validation fixtures prove gateway_state.json cannot report a stale, stopped, or pid-reused process as running.

provenance.note: Hermes defines stale PID and process identity behavior; Gormes owns the Go RuntimeStatusStore and fake process-table tests.

items.name: Token-scoped gateway locks
items.contract: Gateway credential locks are scoped by platform and credential hash so multiple profiles cannot run the same token while unrelated tokens remain independent
items.degraded_mode: Gateway startup/status reports lock-held, stale-lock-cleared, credential-hash-mismatch, and lock-release-failed evidence without deleting unrelated platform locks.
ready_when: Runtime status JSON + PID/process validation can identify stale or live processes without invoking live service managers.
not_ready_when: The slice changes pairing state, gateway status CLI layout, service restart behavior, or stores raw tokens in lock filenames/logs.
acceptance: Lock paths are under the Gormes XDG state root and include platform plus a non-reversible credential hash, never raw token material.
A second process with the same platform/hash is rejected while a different hash or platform can acquire its own lock.
Stale stopped-process locks are cleared only after PID/start-time validation proves the owner is gone.
Release-on-shutdown removes only the current process lock and reports release failures without masking the gateway exit reason.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/gateway/token_lock_test.go plus XDG state-root config coverage. Token-scoped locks now use platform plus full SHA-256 credential hashes in filenames and runtime evidence, reject live same-token owners through fake PID/start-time validation, allow different tokens/platforms independently, clear stopped-process stale locks without deleting unrelated locks, report credential-hash mismatches without overwriting the file, and preserve gateway exit reasons while recording lock-release-failed evidence.
done_signal: Token-lock fixtures prove same-token exclusion, different-token independence, stale cleanup, and safe release without exposing raw credential material.

provenance.note: Hermes defines token-scoped lock semantics; Gormes maps them into XDG state and Go fake-process fixtures.

items.name: Gateway /restart command + takeover markers
items.contract: Gateway /restart uses registry-owned active-turn policy, deduped takeover markers, and service-manager exit semantics without looping redelivered platform updates
items.degraded_mode: Gateway command/status reports restart_requested, takeover_marker_seen, duplicate_restart_suppressed, service_manager_unavailable, and restart_timeout evidence.
ready_when: Drain-timeout resume_pending recovery, Runtime status JSON + PID/process validation, and Token-scoped gateway locks are validated on main.
not_ready_when: The slice opens real service managers in unit tests, changes pairing approval, or implements service update active polling from Phase 5.O in the same change.
acceptance: CommandRegistry includes /restart with a gateway-safe active-turn policy that rejects or queues according to the frozen command semantics.
A restart request writes a takeover marker containing source platform, chat/thread, update/message ID, generation, and timestamp before returning the service-manager exit code.
Redelivered platform events with the same marker are suppressed once and report duplicate_restart_suppressed instead of starting a loop.
--replace/takeover startup consumes the marker, sends at most one restart notification, and clears or expires the marker under fake-clock tests.
items.note: TDD landed: restart-command fixtures lock /restart registry policy, takeover marker write/read/expiry, once-only duplicate redelivery suppression, restart_timeout resume_pending recovery, service-manager unavailable evidence, and EX_TEMPFAIL service-manager exit semantics without live service control.
done_signal: Restart-command fixtures prove registry policy, takeover marker write/read/expiry, duplicate suppression, and service-manager exit semantics without live service control.

provenance.note: Hermes defines restart/takeover behavior; Gormes keeps the first implementation fake-service-manager and marker-store driven.

items.name: Session expiry finalized-flag migration
items.contract: Gateway session metadata migrates legacy memory_flushed state into expiry_finalized evidence without adding new hidden memory-flush writes
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status reports migrated legacy memory_flushed evidence separately from current expiry_finalized state so operators can tell old session records from new finalization writes.
ready_when: Session metadata load/save can be exercised with temp directories and fake clocks without starting Telegram, Discord, Slack, or a live gateway manager.
This slice owns only metadata migration and write-shape evidence; hook execution and retry policy live in the dependent expiry hook row.
not_ready_when: The slice calls Goncho/Honcho extractors, launches a model-driven memory flush, edits pairing/restart behavior, or implements idle scanning.
unblocks: Session expiry hook cleanup retry evidence
acceptance: A legacy session record with memory_flushed=true loads as expiry_finalized=true and records migrated_memory_flushed evidence.
New session metadata writes use expiry_finalized and never emit a memory_flushed field.
Resume/switch-session fixtures prove no memory-flush task is queued or called while reading or rewriting migrated session metadata.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed the session metadata data-shape migration only. Legacy raw metadata with memory_flushed=true decodes as expiry_finalized=true with migrated_memory_flushed evidence, rewrites drop the legacy memory_flushed key, new finalized metadata writes only expiry_finalized, and gateway submit/status fixtures report migrated evidence without queuing memory work. Hermes removed flush_memories; Gormes keeps Goncho memory extraction explicit and independent from gateway expiry metadata.
done_signal: Session metadata fixtures prove legacy memory_flushed records migrate to expiry_finalized and new writes contain no hidden memory-flush fields.

provenance.note: Hermes defines finalization-only session expiry after flush removal; Gormes adapts only the persisted metadata boundary in this slice.

items.name: Session expiry hook cleanup retry evidence
items.contract: Gateway expiry finalization invokes hook and cached-agent cleanup exactly once per expired session, with bounded retry/give-up evidence and no memory flush task
items.degraded_mode: Gateway status reports expiry_finalize_pending, expiry_finalize_failed, and expiry_finalize_gave_up with attempt counts instead of retrying hidden cleanup work forever.
ready_when: Session expiry finalized-flag migration is validated on main.
A fake expiry scanner and fake cleanup hooks can run against temp session metadata without live channel transports.
not_ready_when: The slice changes session key generation, pairing/restart status, live adapter startup, or Goncho/Honcho memory extraction behavior.
acceptance: Expired-session fixtures invoke finalize hooks and cached-agent cleanup exactly once, then persist expiry_finalized evidence across reload.
Transient finalize failures retry for three fake-clock attempts and then mark gave-up evidence so the gateway cannot spin forever.
Resume/switch-session fixtures prove no memory flush task or Goncho/Honcho extractor is started before switching sessions.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed a gateway session-expiry finalization sweep with fakeable scanner/finalizer seams, one-shot successful on_session_finalize-style hooks plus cached-agent cleanup, persisted expiry_finalized metadata across reload, three-attempt retry/give-up evidence in metadata and runtime status, and submit fixtures proving no memory flush, Goncho, or Honcho work is launched on resumed finalized sessions.
done_signal: Expiry hook fixtures prove one-shot cleanup, bounded retry/give-up evidence, persisted finalization state, and zero memory-flush task launches.

provenance.note: Hermes defines session expiry as finalization-only; Gormes keeps cleanup evidence in Go gateway/session metadata while preserving explicit Goncho memory boundaries.

items.name: Channel lifecycle writers into status model
items.note: TDD landed: internal/gateway.RuntimeStatusStore persists gateway_state.json atomically, gateway.Manager writes gateway/channel lifecycle states into that shared model, and focused tests prove read-model coherence for start, stop, and startup-failure paths before pairing UX widens.

drift_state.origin_decision: Hermes restart, pairing, status, and session-expiry finalization donor behavior is now represented by validated Go gateway/session rows. Recent Hermes edc78e25 drift is TUI-focused, not gateway-lifecycle drift, so future restart/session-expiry drift should become new small 2.F.3 rows only when upstream changes this surface again.

items.name: Home channel ownership rules
items.note: TDD: define home-channel selection, explicit override precedence, and per-platform fallback rules before cross-platform delivery code.

items.name: Notify-to delivery routing
items.note: TDD: prove delivery-target expansion from notify-to and home-channel settings before adapter dispatch starts consulting directory state.

items.name: Channel directory atomic persistence + lookup
items.note: TDD: port upstream `gateway/channel_directory.py` as an atomic `channel_directory.json` read model with composite `chat_id:thread_id` keys and human name/type lookup before periodic refresh or operator UX.

items.name: Channel directory refresh + stale-target invalidation
items.note: TDD: add deterministic refresh/stale-target behavior after manager-source persistence exists, so send-target resolution never depends on adapter-specific cache side effects.

items.name: Manager remember-source hook
items.note: TDD: persist allowed inbound SessionSource records through gateway.Manager before periodic refresh or send-target resolution starts depending on the directory.

items.name: Mirror + sticker cache surfaces
items.note: TDD: prove cache key and mirror-write contracts before adapter-specific caches land.

items.name: Steer slash command registry + queue fallback
items.contract: Registry-owned active-turn steering command
items.degraded_mode: Gateway returns visible usage, busy, or queued status instead of dropping steer text when the command cannot run immediately.
ready_when: 2.E.2 is complete and the shared CommandDef registry is stable for gateway commands.
not_ready_when: The implementation tries to inject mid-run prompts instead of only registering /steer and queue fallback behavior.
unblocks: Mid-run steer injection between tool calls
Gateway-handled slash commands bypass active-session guard
acceptance: The shared CommandDef registry exposes /steer with argument validation and preview truncation.
No-running-agent paths queue follow-up guidance instead of dropping it.
Running-agent paths do not inject mid-run guidance until the next slice owns that hook.
items.note: TDD: port upstream PR #12116 `/steer` ingress (see `gateway/run.py` steer handler) as a CommandDef on the shared registry with queue-fallback when no agent is running, explicit missing-argument usage text, and deterministic preview truncation. Keep the mid-run inject hook unimplemented until the next 2.F.5 slice lands.
done_signal: The shared command registry exposes /steer and no-agent paths return visible queued guidance.

items.name: Mid-run steer injection between tool calls
items.note: TDD: expose a running-agent steer hook on the kernel so `/steer <prompt>` lands BETWEEN tool calls (not at turn boundary) via a single deterministic callback; prove ordering against concurrent-tool fan-out and the 2.E.2 cancellation contract. Must fall back to the 2.F.5 queue slice when the kernel has no live tool-call iteration in flight, and must refuse to duplicate prompts already routed through that fallback.

items.name: Gateway-handled slash commands bypass active-session guard
items.note: TDD: mirror upstream PRs #12334 and #511ed4da — registry-resolved gateway slash commands must execute even while an agent is running for that session, without interrupting the running turn and without queuing discardable command text. Keep non-gateway/unknown commands behind the existing busy-state response so only the safe registry surface bypasses the guard; cover both allowed and denied paths with fixture tests before shipping.

3.name: Phase 3 — The Black Box (Memory)
3.deliverable: SQLite + FTS5 + ontological graph + semantic fusion in Go; 3.E closes session visibility, audit trails, decay, and cross-chat/session boundaries

items.name: Read-only bbolt sessions.db -> index.yaml mirror
items.note: TDD: shipped internal/session SessionIndexMirror with fixed YAML shape, lexically ordered bbolt key export, and atomic temp-file rewrite semantics.

items.name: Deterministic mirror refresh without mutating session state
items.note: TDD: shipped a deterministic background refresh loop plus TUI/gateway/telegram runtime wiring, skipping unchanged snapshots while keeping the bbolt session map read-only.

items.name: Append-only JSONL writer + schema
items.note: TDD: shipped internal/audit JSONL append writer with a stable envelope (timestamp/source/session_id/agent_id/tool/args/duration_ms/status/result_size_bytes/error) and order-preserving tests.

items.name: Kernel + delegate_task audit hooks
items.note: TDD: kernel executeToolCalls now emits audit records, and delegated child-tool execution under delegate_task appends the same JSONL schema through the subagent runtime.

items.name: Outcome, duration, and error capture
items.note: TDD: RED/GREEN coverage locks duration_ms, status, and error population for successful and failed tool executions, including delegated child-tool runs.

items.name: gormes session export <id> --format=markdown
items.note: Shipped with Cobra wiring and clean missing-session handling; tests pin CLI shape and not-found behavior.

items.name: Render turns, tool calls, and timestamps from SQLite
items.note: Markdown export now reads SQLite turns, renders deterministic ordering/timestamps, and includes persisted tool-call metadata.

items.name: gormes memory status command
items.note: TDD: shipped `gormes memory status` with a SQLite-backed extractor summary, heuristic worker health, and recent dead-letter rendering.

items.name: Extractor queue depth + dead-letter summary
items.note: TDD: added deterministic dead-letter error aggregation to `gormes memory status`, with fixture-backed queue, summary, and CLI rendering coverage.

items.name: Append-only daily usage.jsonl writer
items.note: TDD landed: internal/insights now appends normalized DailyRollup records to usage.jsonl with schema-locked JSONL lines and deterministic append ordering.

items.name: Session, token, and cost rollups from local runtime
items.note: TDD: shipped fixture-driven daily rollups plus a telemetry.Snapshot bridge for session counts, token totals, cost sums, and per-model breakdowns.

items.name: relationships.last_seen schema + backfill
items.note: TDD landed: schema v3g adds relationships.last_seen, backfills legacy rows from updated_at, and keeps recall decay on COALESCE(NULLIF(last_seen, 0), updated_at) so migrated and fresh rows rank deterministically.

items.name: Relationship writer freshness updates
items.note: TDD landed: graph relationship upserts now advance last_seen on every observation while preserving the legacy updated_at timestamp for structural freshness semantics.

items.name: Deterministic weight attenuation at recall time
items.note: TDD landed: recall-time decay is now wired through RecallDecayHorizonDays, fixed-clock recall_sql coverage, and ranking assertions that preserve raw fence weights while attenuating candidate ordering.

items.name: user_id concept above chat_id
items.note: TDD landed: internal/session now persists per-session source/chat/user metadata, resolves canonical user_id bindings above chat_id, inherits known chat bindings for resumed sessions, and rejects conflicting chat-to-user merges.

items.name: Same-chat default recall fence
items.note: TDD landed: internal/memory recall applies a same-chat default fence to exact, FTS, and semantic seed selection, so named entities from another chat do not leak without explicit scope widening.

items.name: Opt-in user-scope recall + source filters
items.note: TDD landed: cross-chat recall widens only through canonical user_id bindings and optional source allowlists, with unresolved user scope falling back to same-chat behavior.

items.name: Interrupted-turn memory sync suppression
items.contract: Interrupted or cancelled turns cannot flush partial observations into GONCHO or external Honcho-compatible memory
items.degraded_mode: Memory status reports skipped or interrupted sync attempts without promoting partial facts to recall.
ready_when: The turn-finalization path can tell a normal completion from an interrupt, cancellation, or client disconnect.
not_ready_when: The slice rewrites extraction, recall, or provider-plugin storage instead of only gating sync/finalization on interrupted turns.
acceptance: Interrupted turns record a skipped-sync reason and do not create new GONCHO conclusions.
Completed turns still sync/extract normally.
Operator status can distinguish skipped interrupted sync from extractor failures.
items.note: TDD landed: interrupted turns are marked as skipped with reason=interrupted, stay out of extractor and Honcho-compatible turn reads, and create no goncho_conclusions; completed turns still mark ready and extract normally. Fixtures cover internal/memory/interrupted_sync_test.go plus skipped-row guards in the memory session catalog and GONCHO service. Verified with go test ./internal/kernel ./internal/memory ./internal/goncho -count=1.
done_signal: Interrupted-turn fixtures prove partial memory observations are skipped while completed turns still sync.

items.name: Honcho-compatible scope/source tool schema
