items.name: Routing policy and fallback selector
items.contract: Smart model routing is a pure selector over explicit overrides, provider availability, fallback policy, and model metadata before any provider call switches models
items.degraded_mode: Routing status reports metadata gaps, unavailable providers, and disabled fallback routes before changing a turn's model.
ready_when: Context limits, pricing, capabilities, and provider-family metadata are fixture-backed.
not_ready_when: The selector mutates kernel turn state, opens provider network calls, or hides operator-specified model overrides.
acceptance: Explicit per-turn or config overrides win over automatic routing unless invalid.
Fallback choices are deterministic from fixture provider availability and model metadata.
Selector output includes a reason code suitable for telemetry and operator status.
items.note: Complete: TDD added `internal/hermes/model_routing.go` plus `internal/hermes/model_routing_test.go` as a pure selector over turn/config overrides, provider availability, automatic simple-turn routing, fallback policy, and fixture model metadata. Tests prove explicit overrides win unless metadata is invalid, fallback selection is deterministic from availability and metadata, disabled fallback/provider gaps are reported as status codes, and selector output carries telemetry-ready reason codes without provider calls or kernel mutation.
done_signal: Routing selector fixtures prove deterministic model choices and reason codes without provider calls or kernel mutation.

items.name: Per-turn model selection
items.contract: Kernel accepts a per-turn model override, sends it on exactly one hermes.ChatRequest, exposes the active model in RenderFrame during that turn, and falls back to the resident config model on the next turn
items.degraded_mode: If no per-turn model is supplied or the override is blank, the kernel continues using cfg.Model and status frames make the resident model explicit.
ready_when: Routing policy and fallback selector is validated on main.
Kernel tests can use internal/hermes.MockClient to inspect ChatRequest.Model without live provider credentials.
Current main has no PlatformEvent.Model field and still builds ChatRequest with k.cfg.Model, so the first test must be RED before implementation.
not_ready_when: The slice reintroduces Config.ModelSelector, changes provider routing policy, makes provider network calls, mutates cfg.Model globally, or wires context compression history pruning.
acceptance: A PlatformEventSubmit with Model="override-model" causes the outbound hermes.ChatRequest.Model to be override-model for that turn.
A following PlatformEventSubmit with no Model uses the original cfg.Model, proving the override did not mutate resident kernel configuration.
RenderFrame.Model reflects the active override while the turn is connecting/streaming and returns to cfg.Model after the turn settles.
The test stays in internal/kernel with MockClient and does not add provider calls or routing selector changes.
items.note: Complete: TDD added internal/kernel/per_turn_model_test.go and threaded PlatformEvent.Model through Run -> runTurn -> hermes.ChatRequest plus RenderFrame.Model. Blank overrides fall back to Config.Model, the resident config model is not mutated, and the implementation leaves provider routing policy unchanged.
done_signal: internal/kernel/per_turn_model_test.go proves override, fallback, and frame-model behavior without mutating cfg.Model or provider routing.

provenance.note: Hermes defines model switching and provider-routing behavior; Gormes must implement a smaller Go kernel per-turn override that consumes the native routing selector without importing Python runtime.

items.name: Per-turn reasoning effort propagation
items.contract: Kernel and provider request models carry an optional reasoning effort for exactly one turn or session without mutating resident model/provider configuration
items.degraded_mode: Status frames and provider request evidence distinguish unsupported reasoning effort, default reasoning, disabled reasoning, and explicit per-turn/session overrides.
ready_when: Per-turn model selection is validated, so the kernel already has a turn-scoped override path and RenderFrame evidence pattern.
A fake hermes.Client can capture ChatRequest fields without live provider credentials.
not_ready_when: The slice implements slash command parsing, persistent config writes, or provider-specific reasoning UI in the same change.
The slice mutates cfg.Model/cfg.Hermes globally or treats reasoning effort as a model alias.
acceptance: ChatRequest carries optional reasoning effort values none, minimal, low, medium, high, and xhigh with validation evidence.
A per-turn reasoning override is sent on one fake provider request and the next turn falls back to the configured default.
Unsupported providers or absent request fields report explicit degraded evidence instead of silently dropping a requested override.
Existing per-turn model selection fixtures remain green and no provider network calls are introduced.
items.note: Complete: TDD added `internal/hermes/reasoning_effort_request_test.go` and `internal/kernel/per_turn_reasoning_test.go`. The provider request model validates and serializes optional reasoning_effort values none, minimal, low, medium, high, and xhigh; absent defaults omit the request field, invalid values are rejected with evidence, and unsupported provider runtimes report degraded evidence. The kernel threads Config.ReasoningEffort and PlatformEvent.ReasoningEffort through exactly one turn without mutating resident configuration, exposes RenderFrame reasoning evidence, forwards supported overrides/defaults, and falls back to provider/config defaults on later turns.
done_signal: Kernel/provider fixtures prove reasoning effort is turn-scoped, validated, visible in status evidence, and absent from subsequent turns unless explicitly requested.

provenance.note: Hermes owns the reasoning-effort behavior; Gormes needs a smaller typed kernel/provider path before command wiring.

drift_state.origin_decision: Hermes 25ba6a4a added session-scoped reasoning-effort overrides on top of existing model override/routing behavior, and 2c56dce0 tightened custom-provider model switch behavior for named endpoints and configured aliases. Gormes has validated per-turn model selection and pure model routing, but it still lacks a typed reasoning-effort request path before gateway command state can drive provider turns; custom-provider switch preservation is tracked as a smaller 5.O CLI/TUI resolver row, so Smart Model Routing remains upstream-led for the per-turn reasoning slice.

items.name: Anthropic OAuth/keychain credential discovery
items.contract: Anthropic credential discovery prefers OS keychain when present and preserves corrupt local auth state for operator recovery
items.degraded_mode: Auth status reports keychain unavailable, corrupt auth backup, or relogin-required without deleting credentials.
ready_when: Token vault owns XDG-scoped credential files and can expose provider auth status without live credentials.
not_ready_when: The slice silently resets corrupt auth state or reads platform keychains during ordinary unit tests.
acceptance: Fake keychain entries take precedence over JSON auth files when valid.
Malformed auth JSON is preserved to a recoverable backup and surfaces a warning.
Stale OAuth failures return relogin-required status instead of retrying indefinitely.
items.note: Hermes added Anthropic keychain and stale-OAuth coverage during this upstream sync. Gormes should port the behavior as credential-state fixtures after the token vault exists, not inside the Anthropic request adapter.
done_signal: Anthropic auth fixtures prove keychain precedence, corrupt-state preservation, and stale-OAuth relogin status.

items.name: Multi-account auth
items.note: TDD: port `agent/credential_pool.py` account selection and rotation semantics with fake credential records before any provider adapter relies on account pools.

items.name: Google OAuth flow + refresh seam
items.note: TDD: port `agent/google_oauth.py` as a transport-neutral OAuth state machine with device/browser flow fixtures, refresh-token persistence, and cancellation/error cases before Google Code Assist uses it.

items.name: Provider-side resilience
items.contract: Provider resilience umbrella over retry, cache, rate, and budget behavior
items.degraded_mode: Provider and kernel status expose retry schedule, Retry-After hints, cache disabled paths, rate guards, and budget telemetry gaps.
items.fixture: internal/hermes and internal/kernel provider resilience fixtures
ready_when: Provider interface + stream fixture harness is available for resilience fixture coverage.
not_ready_when: The row is used to port every retry, cache, rate, and budget behavior as one monolithic implementation.
acceptance: Retryable and fatal provider errors are classified deterministically.
Kernel retry policy reports schedule and provider hint decisions.
Unavailable cache/rate/budget capability is visible before routing relies on it.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed the 4.H umbrella status contract without porting the sibling slices monolithically. `internal/hermes.ProviderStatusOf` now exposes prompt-cache, rate-guard, and budget-telemetry capability rows for OpenAI-compatible, Anthropic, mock, and unknown providers; OpenAI-compatible request fixtures prove unsupported `cache_control` metadata is stripped with a visible disabled path, while Anthropic reports cache-control support. `internal/kernel.RenderFrame` now carries provider status plus retry status, including the 1s/2s/4s/8s/16s schedule, max Retry-After cap, attempts used, provider hint vs scheduled-backoff decision, and retryable provider-error class/kind. Rate-guard and budget-telemetry implementations remain planned in their sibling slices, but their unavailable state is visible before routing can rely on them. Verified with `go test ./internal/hermes ./internal/kernel -count=1`.
done_signal: Retry, cache, rate, budget, and provider-hint behavior is fixture-covered and visible in provider/kernel status.

items.name: Classified provider-error taxonomy
items.contract: Structured provider error classification contract
items.degraded_mode: Provider status and logs expose auth, rate-limit, context, retryable, and non-retryable classes instead of raw opaque errors.
ready_when: Provider-side resilience remains active and error taxonomy fixtures can be split from retry-policy changes.
not_ready_when: The slice changes kernel retry timing instead of only defining structured error classes and fixtures.
acceptance: HTTP status and provider body hints map to stable error classes.
Auth and context-length failures are never retried as generic transient errors.
Fixture tables cover rate-limit, auth, context, retryable, and fatal cases.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed `internal/hermes.ClassifyProviderError` with stable provider-error kinds for `rate_limit`, `auth`, `context`, `retryable`, `non_retryable`, and `unknown` while preserving the existing coarse `Classify` retry/fatal contract used by the kernel. `internal/hermes/errors_test.go` now fixture-covers HTTP status and provider body hints for rate-limit, auth, context overflow, retryable server/transport failures, non-retryable request failures, and OpenRouter-style wrapped metadata errors without adding Retry-After hint parsing from the sibling slice.
done_signal: Fixture tables classify rate-limit, auth, context, retryable, and fatal provider errors deterministically.

items.name: Unsupported temperature retry + Codex no-temperature guard
items.contract: Provider calls that actually send temperature retry once without it on unsupported-parameter errors, while Codex Responses payload conversion never emits temperature
items.degraded_mode: Provider status records temperature-stripped retry evidence instead of treating unsupported temperature as an opaque fatal 400.
ready_when: Classified provider-error taxonomy and provider transcript fixtures are validated.
Gormes has ChatRequest.Temperature and can exercise fake HTTP providers without live credentials.
not_ready_when: The slice adds provider/model allowlists, retries unrelated 400s, or sends temperature through Codex Responses payloads.
acceptance: Unsupported-temperature detector matches OpenAI/Codex, Copilot, OpenRouter, Anthropic-compatible, and gateway-style phrasings from the Hermes b35d692f tests.

When the first request includes temperature and fails with an unsupported-temperature error, exactly one retry removes only temperature and preserves model, max tokens, tools, messages, and timeout context.
Unrelated 400s and calls that did not send temperature do not trigger the temperature retry path.
Codex Responses request builders prove no temperature field is emitted.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/hermes/unsupported_temperature_retry_test.go. OpenAI-compatible requests now serialize optional max_tokens and temperature when present, retry exactly once without temperature on upstream-matched unsupported-temperature provider errors, preserve the rest of the request body, and record temperature-stripped retry evidence in ProviderStatus. Unrelated 400s and requests that did not send temperature do not retry, while Codex Responses payload fixtures prove the transport mapping never emits temperature.
done_signal: Unsupported-temperature fixtures prove one stripped retry, no retry for unrelated errors, and no temperature in Codex Responses payloads.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the reactive retry behavior; Gormes should encode it in the provider boundary before more adapters emit optional request parameters.

items.name: Codex Responses temperature guard after flush removal
items.contract: Codex Responses payload conversion keeps omitting temperature while removing obsolete memory-flush fixture names, source references, and docs language after Hermes deleted the memory-flush path
items.degraded_mode: Provider status and docs explain no-temperature behavior as a Codex Responses transport rule independent of session-boundary memory behavior.
ready_when: Unsupported temperature retry + Codex no-temperature guard and Generic unsupported-parameter retry + max_tokens guard are validated on main.
The change can be proven by renaming/splitting fake payload fixtures; no live Codex, OAuth, or memory provider is required.
not_ready_when: The slice changes Codex OAuth, provider retries, memory extraction, or Responses streaming behavior instead of only removing flush-specific assumptions from temperature fixtures and docs.
acceptance: Codex Responses request-builder fixtures still prove temperature is never emitted even when ChatRequest.Temperature is set.
Test names, progress source_refs, and generated docs no longer cite the deleted upstream Codex memory-flush test as a current donor.
Unsupported-temperature retry fixtures still cover the Hermes 5006b220 auxiliary task names without the deleted memory-flush auxiliary task.
Progress validation and internal/hermes tests pass without changing runtime retry semantics.
items.note: Complete: TDD split the Codex Responses no-temperature fixture into internal/hermes/codex_responses_temperature_test.go and renamed the planner boundary around a transport-level request mapping rule. The unsupported-temperature retry fixtures now retain the Hermes auxiliary task table for compression, session_search, vision, and web_extract while excluding the deleted memory-flush auxiliary task, and progress source references no longer list the deleted Codex memory-flush donor test.
done_signal: Codex Responses temperature fixtures and generated progress docs explain the no-temperature rule without memory-flush donor references.

provenance.note: Hermes removed the memory-flush fallback; Gormes keeps the transport rule but should rename the test/docs boundary.

items.name: Generic unsupported-parameter retry + max_tokens guard
items.contract: OpenAI-compatible provider calls detect unsupported request parameters by name and retry max_tokens as max_completion_tokens only when the first payload actually sent max_tokens
items.degraded_mode: Provider status records parameter-stripped retry evidence for supported reactive retries instead of surfacing opaque 400s or sending max_completion_tokens when no max_tokens was requested.
ready_when: Unsupported temperature retry + Codex no-temperature guard is validated on main.
Gormes HTTP providers serialize MaxTokens and Temperature through fake HTTP tests without live credentials.
not_ready_when: The slice deletes arbitrary unsupported parameters silently, retries unrelated 400s, or emits max_completion_tokens when MaxTokens is zero/omitted.
blocked_by: Unsupported temperature retry + Codex no-temperature guard
acceptance: A generic isUnsupportedParameterError-style helper matches temperature, max_tokens, seed, and top_p provider phrasings including unsupported, unknown, unrecognized, invalid, and does-not-support markers.
The temperature wrapper delegates to the generic helper and existing unsupported-temperature retry/status fixtures remain green.

When the first request emits max_tokens and receives an unsupported max_tokens error, exactly one retry removes max_tokens, sets max_completion_tokens to the same value, and preserves model, messages, tools, temperature, timeout context, and session headers.
When MaxTokens is zero/omitted or the provider error is unrelated, the client makes one request and never adds max_completion_tokens with a zero or null value.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/hermes/unsupported_parameter_retry_test.go. OpenAI-compatible provider errors now route through a generic unsupported-parameter detector used by the temperature wrapper and covering temperature, max_tokens, seed, and top_p phrasings including unsupported, unknown, unrecognized, invalid, and does-not-support markers. A supported max_tokens recovery retries exactly once only when the original payload emitted max_tokens, rewrites it to max_completion_tokens with the same value, preserves model, messages, tools, temperature, session headers, and caller context, and records UnsupportedParameterRetry evidence in ProviderStatus. Omitted MaxTokens, unrelated max_tokens errors, and unsupported arbitrary parameters do not retry or emit zero/null max_completion_tokens.
done_signal: Unsupported-parameter fixtures prove generic detector coverage, preserved temperature retry behavior, max_tokens-to-max_completion_tokens retry, and no retry when MaxTokens is absent.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the reactive parameter retry behavior; Gormes should keep the port in the OpenAI-compatible provider boundary rather than per-provider allowlists.

items.name: Jittered reconnect backoff schedule
items.note: TDD landed: `internal/kernel/retry.go` RetryBudget applies a 1s/2s/4s/8s/16s +/-20% schedule across five attempts with `retry_test.go` coverage, and the kernel reconnect path consumes it on every open-stream retry. Do not extend this slice; Retry-After plumbing lives in the sibling slices below.

items.name: Retry-After header parsing + HTTPError hint
items.note: Complete: TDD extended `HTTPError` with a `RetryAfter` hint and wired HTTP client response errors through shared parsing. `internal/hermes/client_test.go` covers integer-second `Retry-After`, HTTP-date `Retry-After`, `retry_after` and `retryAfter` JSON body hints when the header is absent, a 16s cap, and deterministic zero-hint behavior.

items.name: Kernel retry honors Retry-After hint
items.note: Complete: TDD added `RetryBudget.NextDelayFor` so open-stream retry consumes the existing retry budget while preferring a provider `RetryAfter` hint when present, capping it at 16s, and falling back to the jittered schedule when absent. `internal/kernel/retry_test.go` covers capped hint behavior, fallback behavior, and a fast end-to-end retry through the kernel.

items.name: Streaming interrupt retry suppression
items.contract: Kernel stream cancellation and /stop-style events abort retry loops before any fresh provider stream is opened
items.degraded_mode: Render/status frames report interrupted retry suppression instead of reconnecting after the operator has cancelled a running turn.
ready_when: Jittered reconnect backoff schedule and Kernel retry honors Retry-After hint are validated on main.
A fake hermes.Client can make the first stream attempt return a retryable error while the test submits PlatformEventCancel or cancels the parent context.
not_ready_when: The slice changes provider error classification, retry timing constants, tool execution cancellation, or gateway command policy instead of only proving retry suppression after cancellation.
acceptance: If PlatformEventCancel arrives during a stream attempt that then returns a retryable error, the kernel enters cancellation/finalization and does not call OpenStream again.
If the parent context is cancelled before reconnect backoff completes, Wait returns immediately and no retry attempt is opened.
A no-cancel control fixture still retries normally after a transient stream error so the fix does not disable Route-B recovery.
Interrupted turns continue to skip memory sync instead of persisting partial assistant content.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed `internal/kernel/stream_interrupt_retry_test.go` so retryable stream setup failures cannot open a fresh provider stream after PlatformEventCancel is queued, parent context cancellation during reconnect backoff suppresses retry, and the no-cancel control still recovers through Route-B retry. The kernel retry wait now drains platform control events before/during reconnect backoff and interrupted turns continue to call SkipMemorySync without finalizing partial assistant content.
done_signal: Kernel interrupt retry fixtures prove cancel-before-retry, context-cancel-during-backoff, no-cancel retry recovery, and interrupted memory-sync suppression.

provenance.note: Hermes exposes the retry-after-stop regression; Gormes should lock the same behavior at the Go kernel retry boundary.

items.name: Prompt-cache capability guard
items.note: TDD: port `agent/prompt_caching.py` model/provider capability checks so cache-control metadata is attached only when the selected provider supports it.

items.name: Provider rate guard + budget telemetry
items.contract: Provider rate guards distinguish true account quota exhaustion from upstream-capacity 429s and expose budget telemetry without sleeping or blocking unrelated models in unit tests
items.degraded_mode: Provider status reports rate_guard_unavailable, nous_rate_limited, nous_upstream_capacity, budget_header_missing, and reset-window evidence instead of silently tripping a global breaker.
ready_when: Provider-side resilience, classified provider-error taxonomy, and Retry-After hint handling are validated, so this row only adds rate-guard/budget decisions and status evidence.
The worker can use fake headers and fake clock fixtures; no live Nous Portal, provider key, or wall-clock sleep is required.
not_ready_when: The slice changes retry timing, provider routing, or model fallback policy instead of only classifying rate-guard state and telemetry evidence.
The slice writes process-global breaker state in unit tests or treats every 429 as account-level quota exhaustion.
blocked_by: Provider rate guard — x-ratelimit header classification
Provider rate guard — degraded-state + last-known-good evidence
acceptance: Headers with remaining=0 and reset window >=60s classify as genuine account rate limit and record redacted reset evidence.
429 responses with healthy x-ratelimit buckets classify as upstream_capacity and do not trip cross-session/provider-wide breaker state.
Last-known-good exhausted bucket evidence can classify a bare 429 as genuine, while healthy last-known state keeps a bare 429 as upstream_capacity.
Malformed or missing budget headers degrade visibly without sleeping, retry amplification, or blocking other provider/model choices.
items.note: Hermes 192e7eb2 fixed Nous Portal upstream-capacity 429s tripping the cross-session breaker. Gormes should port the decision table as provider-status evidence first, not as a live shared-file breaker.
done_signal: Provider rate-guard fixtures prove genuine quota, upstream-capacity, last-known-state, malformed-header, and no-sleep degraded decisions with visible status evidence.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the Nous 429 distinction; Gormes adapts it to native provider status and retry/budget telemetry.

items.name: Provider rate guard — x-ratelimit header classification
items.contract: TDD packet for a missing pure helper that matches Hermes 192e7eb2 reset-window semantics; create exactly two files and do not wire a provider breaker. First add internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard_classification_test.go in package hermes with TestClassify429 table subtests named genuine_quota_1h_reset, short_reset_upstream_capacity, healthy_remaining_upstream_capacity, missing_headers_insufficient, unknown_headers_ignored, and malformed_values_ignored. Then add internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard.go exposing type RateLimitClass string with constants RateLimitGenuineQuota="genuine_quota", RateLimitUpstreamCapacity="upstream_capacity", RateLimitInsufficientEvidence="insufficient_evidence" and Classify429(headers http.Header) RateLimitClass. Read exactly the four current Hermes Nous bucket tags (requests, requests-1h, tokens, tokens-1h) via the paired x-ratelimit-remaining-{tag} and x-ratelimit-reset-{tag} headers; use headers.Values, strings.TrimSpace, strconv.Atoi for remaining, strconv.ParseFloat for reset seconds. No shared breaker state, no time.Now, no sleeps, no retry policy, and no provider routing changes.
items.degraded_mode: Provider status reports rate_guard_classified as one of {genuine_quota, upstream_capacity, insufficient_evidence}; reset-window evidence is parsed for the decision but detailed budget telemetry waits for the dependent row.
ready_when: internal/hermes already compiles; the row creates a new file and a sibling _test.go.
No upstream gate; pure header parsing with synthetic http.Header values.
internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard.go and provider_rate_guard_classification_test.go are absent on main; the worker's first edit is the focused failing test file.
If Classify429 or RateLimitClass already exists in the worker checkout, the worker should run the focused test and update this row instead of creating duplicate constants.
not_ready_when: The slice changes retry timing, provider routing, or model fallback policy.
The slice writes process-global breaker state in unit tests or sleeps to simulate reset windows.
The worker needs to edit internal/hermes/client.go, internal/hermes/errors.go, internal/kernel/retry.go, or any provider routing code to make the tests pass.
unblocks: Provider rate guard — degraded-state + last-known-good evidence
acceptance: TestClassify429_GenuineQuotaRequiresResetWindow (X-RateLimit-Remaining-Requests-1h=0 and X-RateLimit-Reset-Requests-1h=300) returns RateLimitGenuineQuota even when other present buckets are >0.
TestClassify429_ShortResetStaysUpstreamCapacity (remaining=0 and reset=30) returns RateLimitUpstreamCapacity, mirroring Hermes' <60s transient-throttle rule.
TestClassify429_UpstreamCapacityWhenAllBucketsHaveRemaining (any subset of the four buckets >0, none missing-and-parsed-as-zero) returns RateLimitUpstreamCapacity.
TestClassify429_InsufficientEvidenceWhenNoRateHeaders returns the explicit RateLimitInsufficientEvidence constant and the returned string is non-empty.
TestClassify429_IgnoresUnknownHeaders (Retry-After, X-Custom-Foo) preserves the classification driven solely by the four x-ratelimit-remaining-* buckets.
TestClassify429_UnparseableBucketIsNotPresent (X-RateLimit-Remaining-Tokens="abc") with no other rate headers returns RateLimitInsufficientEvidence rather than treating the malformed value as zero.
items.note: Reshape #7 (2026-04-26T18:25:41Z): tightened the no_progress quarantine into a two-file-only header classifier, named the exact table subtests, and added Hermes' _MIN_RESET_FOR_BREAKER_SECONDS source ref. The target files are absent on main, and this remains a pure helper before shared breaker or budget telemetry wiring.
done_signal: internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard_classification_test.go fixtures prove genuine_quota / upstream_capacity / insufficient_evidence classification from the four current Hermes Nous bucket tags, including the >=60s reset-window guard.
internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard.go contains only RateLimitClass constants plus Classify429; no provider client, retry, or routing file changes are required.

items.name: Provider rate guard — degraded-state + last-known-good evidence
items.contract: TDD packet for a missing pure state-transition helper; this row is not selectable until the Classify429 row provides RateLimitClass, and workers must not stub duplicate constants. First add internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard_degraded_test.go in package hermes with TestApplyClassification covering preserved last-known-good on RateLimitInsufficientEvidence, unknown/zero RateLimitClass treated as insufficient evidence, fresh RateLimitGenuineQuota and RateLimitUpstreamCapacity clearing Unavailable and stamping now, and input value immutability. Then add internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard_degraded.go exposing type GuardState struct{LastKnownClass RateLimitClass; LastKnownAt time.Time; Unavailable bool} and ApplyClassification(state GuardState, now time.Time, class RateLimitClass) GuardState. The helper returns a new value, treats zero-value state.LastKnownClass as RateLimitInsufficientEvidence in returned values, imports time, and reuses RateLimitClass from provider_rate_guard.go. No bucket parsing, reset-window parsing, breaker mutation, retry timing, time.Now lookup, goroutine, or provider routing.
items.degraded_mode: Provider status reports last_known_good=present|absent, plus rate_guard_unavailable or budget_header_missing when classification is unsafe; no retry amplification.
ready_when: Provider rate guard — x-ratelimit header classification is validated on main, so this slice can import RateLimitClass from internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard.go without stubbing it.
internal/hermes/provider_rate_guard_degraded.go and provider_rate_guard_degraded_test.go are absent on main; the worker's first edit is the focused failing test file.
not_ready_when: The slice writes a process-global or cross-session breaker.
The slice changes retry policy or treats every 429 as account-level quota exhaustion.
RateLimitClass or Classify429 is absent in the worker checkout; do not create duplicate constants in provider_rate_guard_degraded.go.

acceptance: TestApplyClassification_PreservesLastKnownGoodOnInsufficientEvidence: input state {LastKnownClass:RateLimitGenuineQuota, LastKnownAt:t0, Unavailable:false} with class=RateLimitInsufficientEvidence returns {RateLimitGenuineQuota, t0, true} (LastKnownAt unchanged).
TestApplyClassification_RecordsClassificationOnGenuineQuota: zero-value input + class=RateLimitGenuineQuota at now=t1 returns {RateLimitGenuineQuota, t1, false}.
TestApplyClassification_RecordsClassificationOnUpstreamCapacity: zero-value input + class=RateLimitUpstreamCapacity at now=t1 returns {RateLimitUpstreamCapacity, t1, false}.
TestApplyClassification_DoesNotMutateInputState: passing a struct value and checking the original is byte-equal after the call (no pointer escapes, no aliasing).
TestApplyClassification_TogglesUnavailableBackToFalseOnFreshEvidence: input {RateLimitGenuineQuota, t0, true} with class=RateLimitUpstreamCapacity returns Unavailable=false.

TestApplyClassification_UnknownClassIsInsufficientEvidence: input {RateLimitUpstreamCapacity, t0, false} with class=RateLimitClass("") preserves LastKnownClass/LastKnownAt and returns Unavailable=true.
items.note: Reshape #7 (2026-04-26T18:25:41Z): tightened the no_progress quarantine by making the RateLimitClass prerequisite explicit and forbidding duplicate constants/stubs. The row remains independent from budget telemetry, shared breaker state, and retry policy.

items.name: Discord tool split + platform-scoped toolsets

Gormes now exposes split discord and discord_admin descriptors only when the Discord or gateway platform scope explicitly enables those default-off toolsets, drops schemas for missing tokens, missing privileged intents, empty allowlists, or disallowed platforms, filters stale action-specific descriptions and parameters from dynamic schemas, preserves server_actions allowlist config, and keeps legacy discord_server out of active descriptors without porting Discord REST handlers.

items.contract: Terminal/process tools rate-limit watch-pattern notifications, suppress matches after process exit, and make notify_on_complete mutually exclusive with watch_patterns before any live process runner is exposed
items.degraded_mode: Tool status reports watch-pattern disabled, promoted-to-notify-on-complete, or global overflow evidence instead of flooding operators with duplicate delayed process notifications.
ready_when: Tool registry inventory + schema parity harness is validated on main.
The first slice owns a pure fake-clock notification policy under internal/tools and does not need to spawn a real shell or wire the terminal tool handler.
not_ready_when: The slice opens a PTY, starts real subprocesses, ports the whole process_registry, or combines notification throttling with file/checkpoint/session tool migration.
blocked_by: Tool registry inventory + schema parity harness
unblocks: Hardline command pattern table + DetectHardline function
acceptance: notify_on_complete=true with background=true drops watch_patterns and returns operator-readable evidence; non-background calls and watch-only calls keep their requested behavior.
A running fake session emits at most one watch_match per 15 seconds; suppressed matches in the same window count as one strike.
After three consecutive strike windows the session disables watch_patterns, promotes to notify_on_complete semantics, and emits one watch_disabled summary.
Matches arriving after session.exited are ignored, and a global fake-registry breaker suppresses more than 15 watch events in 10 seconds across sessions with trip/release summary evidence.
items.note: Complete: TDD landed internal/tools/process_notifications_test.go and a pure fake-clock process notification policy. The fixture proves notify_on_complete/watch_patterns mutual exclusion with operator evidence, per-session 1/15s watch throttling, three-strike watch disablement with notify_on_complete promotion, suppress-after-exit behavior, and global overflow trip/release summaries without spawning processes.
done_signal: Process-notification fixtures prove mutual exclusion, per-session 1/15s throttling, three-strike promotion, suppress-after-exit, and global overflow trip/release behavior without spawning processes.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the watch-pattern flood-control behavior; Gormes should port it as Go-native process-tool policy before exposing a terminal runner.

drift_state.origin_decision: Hermes still leads terminal/process tool behavior. The 97d54f0e watch-pattern flood-control fix must become a small Go-native policy fixture before Gormes ports the live process runner or broad stateful tool migration queue.

items.name: Docker backend top-level container reuse semantics
items.contract: Pure helper internal/tools/docker_container_key.go exposes type DockerContainerRequest struct{TaskID string; IsSubagent bool; IsRollout bool} and DockerContainerKey(req DockerContainerRequest) string. The helper trims TaskID, returns "default" for top-level requests with empty TaskID, returns the trimmed TaskID for top-level explicit task IDs, and returns the trimmed TaskID for subagent or rollout requests. If IsSubagent or IsRollout is true and TaskID is empty, it returns "" so callers must generate an isolated task ID before creating a Docker environment. No Docker CLI calls, no filesystem reads, no cleanup, no env config.
items.degraded_mode: Doctor/status reports docker_task_scope_missing when an isolated subagent or rollout request lacks a generated task_id instead of silently falling back to the shared default container.
ready_when: internal/tools already exists and can accept a pure helper without the live Docker backend implementation.
The worker can prove behavior with table tests only; no docker binary, container runtime, or live filesystem sandbox is required.
not_ready_when: The slice shells out to docker, creates containers, writes sandbox directories, implements cleanup, or changes execute_code behavior.
The slice treats /new, /reset, or TUI session changes as new Docker task IDs for the top-level agent.
acceptance: TestDockerContainerKey_TopLevelDefault: empty TaskID with IsSubagent=false and IsRollout=false returns "default".
TestDockerContainerKey_TopLevelExplicitTaskID: TaskID="manual" returns "manual" for top-level requests.
TestDockerContainerKey_SubagentRequiresIsolatedTaskID: IsSubagent=true with empty TaskID returns "" and with TaskID="subagent-1" returns "subagent-1".
TestDockerContainerKey_RolloutRequiresIsolatedTaskID: IsRollout=true with empty TaskID returns "" and with TaskID="rollout-1" returns "rollout-1".
TestDockerContainerKey_TrimsWhitespace: surrounding whitespace never creates a distinct container key.
items.note: Hermes 9be83728 clarified that the top-level Docker terminal backend reuses one default container across sessions, /new, and /reset, while delegate_task subagents and RL rollouts isolate by task_id. Gormes should freeze the keying rule before implementing the live Docker backend so public docs and runtime behavior do not diverge.
done_signal: internal/tools/docker_container_key_test.go fixtures prove shared top-level default container keying and isolated subagent/rollout task_id requirements without live Docker.

provenance.note: Upstream clarified Docker lifecycle semantics in docs; Gormes adapts the same contract as a pure keying helper before live container execution lands.

Gormes has only local execute_code today, so Phase 5.B stays upstream-led and now carries a pure container-keying row before live Docker execution.

items.name: Browser hybrid private-URL local sidecar routing
items.contract: Pure routing helper internal/tools/browser_hybrid_routing.go exposes type BrowserRoute struct{SessionKey string; ForceLocal bool; Reason string}, IsPrivateBrowserHost(host string) bool, and RouteBrowserNavigation(taskID, rawURL string, cloudConfigured, autoLocalForPrivateURLs, cdpOverride, camofoxMode bool) BrowserRoute. When cloudConfigured && autoLocalForPrivateURLs && !cdpOverride && !camofoxMode and rawURL's host is localhost, loopback, RFC1918 IPv4, IPv6 loopback, IPv4 link-local, or suffix .local/.lan/.internal, return SessionKey="<taskID-or-default>::local", ForceLocal=true, Reason="private_url_local_sidecar". Public URLs and disabled/override cases return the bare taskID/default, ForceLocal=false. No browser startup, DNS resolution, network calls, chromedp/Rod imports, global session maps, cleanup logic, or config loading in this slice.
items.degraded_mode: Until browser runtime exists, browser status can report hybrid_routing_unavailable; once wired, private URLs must never be sent to cloud providers when auto-local routing is enabled.
ready_when: internal/tools is the current home for tool helper contracts, and no native browser runtime has to exist before a pure route decision helper can land.
The worker can use synthetic URLs and booleans; no live Chromium, Browserbase, Firecrawl, Camofox, DNS, or network dependency is required.
not_ready_when: The slice starts a browser, resolves hostnames over DNS, adds browser config loading, stores session state, edits cleanup behavior, or chooses chromedp/Rod.
The slice weakens post-redirect SSRF blocking; this row only decides the pre-navigation session key for the initial URL.
acceptance: TestRouteBrowserNavigation_PrivateHostsUseLocalSidecar covers localhost, 127.0.0.1, 10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 169.254.x, ::1, and *.local/*.lan/*.internal returning default::local or task::local.
TestRouteBrowserNavigation_PublicURLsUseCloudKey covers github.com and 8.8.8.8 returning the bare task ID with ForceLocal=false.
TestRouteBrowserNavigation_DisabledOrOverrideCases covers no cloud provider, autoLocalForPrivateURLs=false, cdpOverride=true, and camofoxMode=true all returning the bare task ID.
TestRouteBrowserNavigation_DefaultTaskID proves empty task IDs normalize to default and local sidecars use default::local.
The helper uses net/url, net/netip or net.ParseIP, and strings only; no browser runtime package or network/DNS call appears in the file.
items.note: Hermes 42c076d3 added hybrid browser routing so cloud browser providers never receive LAN/localhost URLs and a local Chromium sidecar handles those navigations. Gormes should first freeze the pure route-key decision before choosing a native browser backend.
done_signal: Browser hybrid routing fixtures prove private/LAN hosts select a ::local sidecar only in cloud+auto-local mode, while public and override cases keep the bare session key without starting a browser.

provenance.note: Direct upstream browser behavior drift; Gormes keeps it as a small Go tool-routing helper before native browser runtime work.

drift_state.origin_decision: Hermes 42c076d3 added browser cloud/local hybrid routing for private URLs. Gormes still lacks native browser runtime wiring, so Phase 5.C stays upstream-led and now carries a pure route-key helper row before browser provider bridge work.

items.name: Skills hub search read-model function over registry providers
items.contract: internal/skills exposes Search(ctx, query, providers []HubRegistryProvider, opts) (HubSearchResponse, error) returning sorted results plus typed degraded evidence (HubSearchEvidenceEmptyQuery, HubSearchEvidenceRegistryUnavailable, HubSearchEvidenceRateLimited, HubSearchEvidenceNoResults) without touching the active/inactive skill store or any HTTP client
items.degraded_mode: Search returns HubSearchResponse{Results,Evidence} where Evidence is a typed enum value; callers must not assume an empty Results slice means failure.

The slice can use only the in-memory fake provider; no gateway command wiring, no install flow, and no prompt-injection are part of this row.
not_ready_when: The slice wires Search into internal/gateway/commands.go, downloads registry content, mutates the active store, or adds signature verification.
The slice introduces network calls, OS sleeps, or live time-based behaviour.

acceptance: TestHubSearchEmptyQueryReturnsEmptyEvidence: Search(ctx, '', providers, opts) returns Evidence=HubSearchEvidenceEmptyQuery and no Results.
TestHubSearchSortsAndDedupes: results from two providers are merged, deduped by InstallID, and sorted by Score descending then Name ascending.
TestHubSearchRegistryUnavailable: when one provider returns ErrRegistryUnavailable, Search includes the other provider's results and reports Evidence=HubSearchEvidenceRegistryUnavailable.
TestHubSearchNoResults: a non-empty query with no matches returns Evidence=HubSearchEvidenceNoResults.
Search makes no calls to internal/gateway, internal/skills/store, or any HTTP client (verified by reading hub_search.go imports).

items.name: [IMPORTANT:] prompt prefix for cron and skill commands
items.contract: internal/cron.CronHeartbeatPrefix and internal/skills.BuildSkillSlashCommandMessage emit `[IMPORTANT:` instead of `[SYSTEM:` so Azure OpenAI Default/DefaultV2 content filters do not reject Gormes prompts as prompt-injection (HTTP 400) — same semantic meta-instruction, different bracketed marker; tests update in lockstep so the byte-match assertions still cover drift
items.degraded_mode: Operator-visible prompt text changes from `[SYSTEM: ...]` to `[IMPORTANT: ...]`; behavior is otherwise identical, including the `[SILENT]` suppression contract and the skill body trimming.
ready_when: Upstream Hermes shipped this rename across two commits (d7a34682 + 20cb706e) on 2026-04-09 / 2026-04-26 with explicit cause (Azure content filter HTTP 400 on `[SYSTEM:` markers).
Gormes uses the same marker pattern in exactly two production code paths today: internal/cron/heartbeat.go (CronHeartbeatPrefix) and internal/skills/commands.go (BuildSkillSlashCommandMessage).
not_ready_when: The slice changes the `[SILENT]` token semantics, the skill body trimming, the cron prompt structure beyond the bracketed marker word, or the `Heartbeat [SYSTEM:] + [SILENT] delivery contract` row name in 2.D (that row name is a historical record; only the runtime constant + the byte-match tests change).
The slice introduces a new Azure provider adapter, a content-filter-detection layer, or a configurable marker word — the change is a hardcoded literal rename only.

The slice updates internal/progress/progress_test.go literal assertions for the 2.D row name (the row name in progress.json must stay as `Heartbeat [SYSTEM:] + [SILENT] delivery contract` for historical accuracy).
acceptance: internal/cron/heartbeat.go:CronHeartbeatPrefix starts with `[IMPORTANT:` (replacing `[SYSTEM:`) and the load-bearing phrases (`DELIVERY:`, `SILENT:`, `[SILENT]`) are byte-identical to the prior version.

internal/cron/heartbeat_test.go asserts `strings.HasPrefix(full, "[IMPORTANT:")` (not `[SYSTEM:`); the existing TestHeartbeatPrefix_ContainsLoadBearingPhrases load-bearing phrase set updates only its first member.
internal/skills/commands.go:BuildSkillSlashCommandMessage emits `[IMPORTANT: The user has invoked the "<name>" skill, ...` (replacing `[SYSTEM:`).
internal/skills/preprocessing_commands_test.go updates its expected golden string to `[IMPORTANT:` for the affected fixtures.
DetectSilent semantics in internal/cron/heartbeat.go are unchanged (the `[SILENT]` token is independent of the leading marker).
items.note: Mirror of Hermes commits d7a34682 + 20cb706e (Azure content filter dodge). Workers must NOT rename the historical 2.D row `Heartbeat [SYSTEM:] + [SILENT] delivery contract` — that row is complete and its name reflects the original landing. The runtime constant + tests rename in lockstep. cli.py, gateway/run.py, and tools/process_registry.py do not have direct Gormes analogs that emit this marker today, so this slice covers only the cron + skills constants.
done_signal: go test ./internal/cron and ./internal/skills both pass after the marker rename; `grep -rn '\[SYSTEM:' internal/cron/ internal/skills/` returns no matches in production code; `grep -rn '\[IMPORTANT:' internal/cron/ internal/skills/` returns at least 4 matches (constant + tests in both packages).

provenance.note: Direct port of Hermes commits d7a34682 (initial rename in cron/skill_commands) + 20cb706e (extension to cli/gateway/process_registry). Gormes only mirrors the cron + skills changes because the cli/gateway/process_registry call sites don't emit this marker yet.

items.name: Skills list — enabled/disabled status column + --enabled-only filter
items.contract: internal/skills/list.go exposes type SkillStatus string ("enabled", "disabled"), extends SkillRow with a Status field and adds ListOptions{Source string; EnabledOnly bool}. ListInstalledSkills(opts ListOptions, disabled map[string]struct{}) []SkillRow returns every installed skill annotated with Status from the disabled set; when opts.EnabledOnly is true, disabled rows are filtered out. The CLI surface (gormes skills list --source <s> --enabled-only) calls this helper and prints a table with a Status column plus a summary "N enabled, M disabled" (or "K enabled shown" when --enabled-only). No platform-aware override read in this slice — disabled set comes from the active profile only, mirroring upstream do_list semantics.
items.degraded_mode: Status column makes disabled skills visible without forcing the operator to read config; --enabled-only matches the upstream "what will load" introspection question.
ready_when: internal/skills already lists installed skills (existing list.go or equivalent) and has a typed disabled-skill set the active-profile config exposes.
CLI table rendering exists for skills already (status column is an additional column).
not_ready_when: The slice plumbs a HERMES_PLATFORM-style platform override into list — upstream test_do_list_platform_env_is_ignored asserts the platform arg stays nil here.
The slice rewrites do_check, do_install, or do_search behavior.
unblocks: Skills hub search read-model function over registry providers
acceptance: TestListInstalledSkills_StatusColumnPopulated annotates every row with Status="enabled" when disabled is empty.
TestListInstalledSkills_DisabledRowsCarryDisabledStatus marks rows whose name is in the disabled set as Status="disabled".
TestListInstalledSkills_EnabledOnlyFilter hides disabled rows when opts.EnabledOnly is true.
TestListInstalledSkills_SourceFilterRespected restricts rows to the requested source ("hub"|"builtin"|"local").
TestSkillsListCommand_RendersStatusColumnAndSummary prints the Status column and "N enabled, M disabled" footer (or "K enabled shown" with --enabled-only).
TestSkillsListCommand_PlatformArgNotPropagated proves the disabled-set lookup does not pass a platform override.
items.note: Upstream-sync row — Hermes PR #16129 (commit 0e2a53ea, 2026-04-26) added "skills list --enabled-only" so operators can answer "which skills will actually load for this profile?" without reading config. Gormes should match the introspection contract; the platform-arg guard test is intentional — Hermes -p swaps HERMES_HOME, so per-platform overrides must NOT leak in via env.
done_signal: internal/skills/list_test.go and internal/cli/skills_command_test.go fixtures prove the Status column, --enabled-only filter, and platform-arg guard.

provenance.note: Mirrors PR #16129; pure introspection feature — no skill execution change.

Gormes does not have a static feeds category label in internal/skills; the skills surface remains upstream-led for hub search/list/install parity, but no new executable row is needed until a Gormes-generated skills catalog grows empty category markers.

items.name: MCP server config/env resolver
items.contract: Gormes parses Hermes-compatible mcp_servers config into safe stdio/HTTP server definitions with env validation, timeout defaults, header redaction, and Honcho MCP self-hosted URL support before any live MCP connection starts
items.degraded_mode: MCP status reports disabled, invalid transport, invalid env, missing SDK, or secret-redacted config errors instead of launching partial servers.
ready_when: Platform toolset config persistence + MCP sentinel is validated on main, so unknown MCP server names and no_mcp suppression already round-trip safely.
This slice can use in-memory YAML/JSON fixtures and must not import an MCP SDK or spawn subprocesses.
not_ready_when: The slice opens stdio/HTTP transports, performs OAuth, probes tools, starts managed gateways, or treats HONCHO_API_URL as global Goncho configuration instead of a server-local MCP env binding.
unblocks: MCP OAuth state store + noninteractive auth errors
acceptance: Config fixtures parse stdio servers with command, args, env, timeout, connect_timeout, and sampling fields.
HTTP fixtures parse url, headers, timeout, and connect_timeout while redacting Authorization and token-like values from status/errors.
Invalid env var names, servers with both command and url, and servers with neither command nor url fail before runtime startup.
A Honcho MCP fixture preserves HONCHO_API_URL only as the configured server env value and never exposes it as a request header or Goncho memory setting.
items.note: Complete: TDD added a pure internal/tools MCP config resolver without SDK imports or runtime startup. Fixtures prove Hermes-compatible YAML/JSON parsing for stdio command/args/env/sampling and timeout defaults, HTTP url/header parsing with Authorization/token-like redaction, fail-closed invalid env/transport/missing env/missing SDK statuses without valid server output, and Honcho HONCHO_API_URL preserved only as a server-local MCP env binding while config.Load keeps Goncho memory defaults independent.

items.name: MCP stdio transport + tool/list discovery
items.contract: internal/tools/mcp_stdio.go exposes a stdio MCP client (NewStdioClient(def MCPServerDefinition, opts StdioClientOpts) (*StdioClient, error); (*StdioClient).Initialize(ctx) error; (*StdioClient).ListTools(ctx) ([]MCPRawTool, error); (*StdioClient).Close() error) that speaks JSON-RPC over a stdio.ReadWriteCloser injected from tests — deterministic over an in-process fake server with no real subprocess
items.degraded_mode: Initialize/ListTools failures return typed errors (ErrConnectTimeout, ErrInitializeFailed, ErrInvalidJSONRPCResponse) and Close is idempotent; no global stderr/stdout writes, no os.StartProcess in tests.
ready_when: internal/tools/mcp_config.go already provides MCPServerDefinition with Command/Args/Env and the stdio transport constant.
Tests inject a `Conn io.ReadWriteCloser` instead of spawning a real subprocess; an in-process bytes.Buffer-based fake server can echo JSON-RPC frames.
not_ready_when: The slice opens a real OS subprocess in tests, talks to the network, implements the HTTP transport, performs schema normalization beyond raw tool envelopes, or registers tools into the kernel registry.
The slice introduces OAuth, sampling, structured-content adapters, or `tools/list_changed` notification handling.
acceptance: internal/tools/mcp_stdio.go declares: type StdioClientOpts struct{ Conn io.ReadWriteCloser, Logger *slog.Logger, Now func() time.Time }; type StdioClient struct{ ... }; type MCPRawTool struct{ Name, Description string, InputSchema json.RawMessage }; var ErrConnectTimeout, ErrInitializeFailed, ErrInvalidJSONRPCResponse error.
TestStdioClient_InitializeNegotiatesProtocolVersion writes a fake `initialize` response with protocolVersion `2024-11-05` and asserts Initialize returns nil + records the negotiated version.
TestStdioClient_InitializeReturnsErrInitializeFailedOnError writes a fake JSON-RPC error response and asserts Initialize returns ErrInitializeFailed wrapped with the error message.

TestStdioClient_ListToolsParsesToolList writes a fake `tools/list` response with two tools (one with InputSchema, one without) and asserts ListTools returns both with stable ordering and verbatim InputSchema bytes.
TestStdioClient_ConnectTimeoutHonoredViaContext sets ctx with a 10ms deadline against a fake that never responds and asserts ListTools returns context.DeadlineExceeded (or wrapped ErrConnectTimeout).
TestStdioClient_CloseIsIdempotent calls Close twice and asserts no panic, no double-close error.
No test calls os/exec; verified by `grep -L 'os/exec' internal/tools/mcp_stdio.go internal/tools/mcp_stdio_test.go`.
items.note: Reshaped 2026-04-26 (split 1 of 3 from the prior `MCP fake-server discovery + tool schema normalization` quarantine). Original contract bundled stdio + HTTP + normalization + dynamic refresh + stderr isolation into one medium row; workers couldn't land it atomically and reported no_progress 3 times. This row is the smallest runnable stdio slice. The original RowHealth was dropped per the split convention.
done_signal: All 5 TestStdioClient_* fixtures pass and `grep -L 'os/exec' internal/tools/mcp_stdio*.go` confirms no real subprocess is spawned.

provenance.note: Hermes remains the donor for MCP stdio runtime behavior; Gormes ports it behind an injected io.ReadWriteCloser so unit tests skip the real subprocess. Health block dropped per split convention.

items.name: MCP HTTP transport + tool/list discovery
items.contract: internal/tools/mcp_http.go exposes an HTTP MCP client (NewHTTPClient(def MCPServerDefinition, opts HTTPClientOpts) (*HTTPClient, error); (*HTTPClient).Initialize(ctx) error; (*HTTPClient).ListTools(ctx) ([]MCPRawTool, error); (*HTTPClient).Close() error) that speaks JSON-RPC over an injectable http.RoundTripper — deterministic over httptest.Server in unit tests with no real network egress
items.degraded_mode: Same typed errors as the stdio client (ErrConnectTimeout, ErrInitializeFailed, ErrInvalidJSONRPCResponse) plus ErrAuthRequired (HTTP 401) so the OAuth follow-up row can build on it; no panic on response close even if the server hangs up mid-stream.
ready_when: `MCP stdio transport + tool/list discovery` is complete (MCPRawTool and the typed error set are defined).
MCPServerDefinition.URL + Headers are present in mcp_config.go for HTTP servers.
Tests construct an httptest.Server returning canned JSON-RPC responses; no real network destination is used.
not_ready_when: The slice talks to a real network endpoint, implements OAuth flows, performs schema normalization, or wires the HTTP client into the kernel tool registry.
The slice changes the MCPRawTool struct shape introduced by the stdio row.

acceptance: internal/tools/mcp_http.go declares: type HTTPClientOpts struct{ Transport http.RoundTripper, Logger *slog.Logger, Now func() time.Time }; type HTTPClient struct{ ... }; var ErrAuthRequired error.
TestHTTPClient_InitializeSendsAuthHeadersFromConfig sets MCPServerDefinition.Headers={`Authorization`:`Bearer test`} and asserts the recorded httptest request includes that header.

TestHTTPClient_ListToolsParsesResponse uses httptest with a canned JSON-RPC tools/list response and asserts ListTools returns the same MCPRawTool shape as the stdio row's TestStdioClient_ListToolsParsesToolList expects.
TestHTTPClient_401ReturnsErrAuthRequired uses httptest returning 401 and asserts ListTools returns ErrAuthRequired (wrapping the response status).

items.name: MCP schema normalization + structured-content adapter
items.contract: internal/tools/mcp_normalize.go converts MCPRawTool slices and tools/call results into native tool descriptors and model-facing strings — sanitizes tool names, validates InputSchema is a JSON-Schema object, flattens structured content vs text content into a single string, isolates server stderr to a per-server log path, and emits a `tools/list_changed`-style refresh hook so dynamic discovery updates replace stale descriptors deterministically
items.degraded_mode: Invalid InputSchema produces a SchemaRejected status (no descriptor registered); structured content with unknown content kinds renders the text fallback; stderr writes are buffered into a bounded ring buffer (8 KiB tail) and flushed to the configured per-server log file or discarded when no path is set.
ready_when: `MCP stdio transport + tool/list discovery` and `MCP HTTP transport + tool/list discovery` are both complete (MCPRawTool exists and both clients return a stable shape).
internal/tools/tool.go provides the native Tool descriptor that NormalizeTools targets.
not_ready_when: The slice depends on a live MCP server, implements OAuth, registers tools into the kernel registry, or changes the kernel.Tools type.
The slice introduces a separate `mcpadapter` package outside internal/tools.
acceptance: internal/tools/mcp_normalize.go declares: type NormalizedTool struct{ Name, ServerName, Description string, InputSchema json.RawMessage, SourceRaw MCPRawTool }; type NormalizeResult struct{ Tools []NormalizedTool, Rejected []SchemaRejection }; type SchemaRejection struct{ ServerName, ToolName, Reason string }; func NormalizeTools(serverName string, raw []MCPRawTool) NormalizeResult; type StructuredContent struct{ ... }; func RenderToolCallResult(parts []StructuredContent) string; type StderrSink interface{ Write(p []byte) (int, error); Close() error }; func NewBoundedStderrSink(path string, tailBytes int) StderrSink.

TestNormalizeTools_SanitizesUnsafeNames maps `weather/get current` to `weather_get_current` (matching upstream sanitize_mcp_name_component semantics) and asserts the original is preserved on SourceRaw.

TestNormalizeTools_RejectsInvalidInputSchema feeds a tool with InputSchema `"true"` (not an object) and asserts NormalizeResult.Rejected contains it with Reason=`input_schema_must_be_object`; the tool is NOT in NormalizeResult.Tools.

TestRenderToolCallResult_StructuredContent renders mixed text+image+resource content and asserts the rendered string includes the text part verbatim, image parts as `[image: <mime>]`, and resource parts as `[resource: <uri>]`.
TestRenderToolCallResult_UnknownContentKindFallsBackToText feeds a content kind `unknown_xyz` and asserts the renderer returns its text field if any (no panic, no leak of raw protocol envelope).
TestBoundedStderrSink_TruncatesAtTailBytes writes 32 KiB and asserts the file on disk contains only the last 8 KiB (with a `[truncated <N> bytes]` marker prefix).
TestBoundedStderrSink_DiscardModeNoFileWrite passes path=`` and asserts no file is created and Write returns the full byte count without error.
items.note: Reshaped 2026-04-26 (split 3 of 3). Closes the tool/list_changed dynamic-refresh story by giving callers a single NormalizeTools/RenderToolCallResult pair plus a bounded stderr sink. Live `tools/list_changed` notification handling lands in a follow-up Managed tool gateway bridge row once the kernel registry exposes the right hook.
done_signal: All TestNormalizeTools_*, TestRenderToolCallResult_*, and TestBoundedStderrSink_* fixtures pass and `grep -L 'net\.\|http\.' internal/tools/mcp_normalize.go` confirms the normalizer has no transport dependency.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the structured-content + sanitization semantics; Gormes ports them behind a transport-free normalizer so dynamic-refresh tests use the same code path. Health block dropped per split convention.

items.name: MCP OAuth state store + noninteractive auth errors
items.contract: internal/tools adds a pure in-memory MCPOAuthStore with methods Get(server string) (MCPOAuthToken, bool), Set(server string, tok MCPOAuthToken) error, Clear(server string), and StatusFor(server string, now time.Time) MCPOAuthStatus where MCPOAuthToken has fields {AccessToken, RefreshToken, Scope, Issuer string; ExpiresAt time.Time}, MCPOAuthStatus has fields {Server, State, Evidence string} with State in {'absent','valid','expired','noninteractive_required'}; the store never logs or returns AccessToken/RefreshToken in its String()/Status output
items.degraded_mode: Status output reports state and evidence labels (absent, valid, expired, noninteractive_required) instead of leaking secret material; redaction is enforced in the store boundary, not at call sites.
ready_when: internal/tools already exposes mcp_config.go and mcp_config_test.go, so adding a single new file with one exported store + token type compiles cleanly alongside them.

This slice only defines an in-memory store and a deterministic status read; no token persistence to disk, no refresh client, no HTTP transport, no real OAuth flow, and no browser or device code is required.
not_ready_when: The slice opens HTTP listeners, calls real OAuth issuers, persists tokens to disk, ports refresh logic, registers MCP tool handlers, or modifies internal/tools/mcp_config.go.
The slice's Status output includes any character of an AccessToken or RefreshToken field.
acceptance: TestMCPOAuthStore_AbsentByDefault: NewMCPOAuthStore().StatusFor('srv', t0) returns {Server:'srv', State:'absent', Evidence:'no_token'}.
TestMCPOAuthStore_SetGetRoundTrip: after Set('srv', tok), Get('srv') returns the same token and ok=true; subsequent Clear('srv') makes Get return ok=false and StatusFor returns State='absent'.
TestMCPOAuthStore_ExpiredWhenPastExpiresAt: token with ExpiresAt=t0.Add(-time.Minute) returns StatusFor State='expired', Evidence='token_expired'.
TestMCPOAuthStore_ValidWhenFutureExpiresAt: token with ExpiresAt=t0.Add(time.Hour) returns StatusFor State='valid', Evidence='ok'.

TestMCPOAuthStore_StatusRedactsSecrets: tokens with AccessToken='sk-abc-secret' and RefreshToken='rt-xyz-secret' produce a StatusFor result whose String() and Evidence fields contain neither 'sk-abc-secret' nor 'rt-xyz-secret' (sub-string assertion).

TestMCPOAuthStore_NoninteractiveErrorClass: a constant ErrMCPOAuthNoninteractiveRequired is exported and StatusFor for an absent server in noninteractive mode (NewMCPOAuthStore().WithNoninteractive(true)) returns State='noninteractive_required', Evidence='noninteractive_auth_unavailable'.
items.note: Reshape of the prior quarantined 5.G row. The previous contract spanned token store + refresh flow + 401 recovery + browser handling — workers had no obvious atomic edit. This row narrows scope to one in-memory store with a typed token + status read, six named tests, and one declared error class, all inside internal/tools. Refresh, 401 recovery, and persistence land in follow-up rows. The split intentionally drops the prior health/quarantine block; the contract is materially replaced.
done_signal: internal/tools/mcp_oauth_store.go declares MCPOAuthStore, MCPOAuthToken, MCPOAuthStatus, and ErrMCPOAuthNoninteractiveRequired; six named tests pass; no other internal/tools file is modified.

provenance.note: Reshape of the prior 5.G quarantine. Hermes defines MCP OAuth state shape; Gormes lands the smallest in-memory store + redaction guarantee first, before any refresh, persistence, 401 recovery, or browser logic.

items.name: MCP OAuth refresh + 401 session-expired recovery
items.contract: internal/tools adds a pure function `RefreshMCPOAuth(ctx context.Context, store *MCPOAuthStore, server string, refresher MCPRefresher, now time.Time) (MCPOAuthRefreshResult, error)` where MCPRefresher is an interface with one method `Refresh(ctx, refreshToken string) (newTokens MCPOAuthToken, err error)`; the function refreshes expired tokens, handles 401-equivalent SessionExpired errors by clearing the stored token, and never blocks on user input
items.degraded_mode: Refresh result reports Outcome in {'refreshed','still_valid','token_cleared','noninteractive_required','refresher_unavailable'} so callers can decide whether to retry the underlying MCP call without inspecting transport errors.
ready_when: MCP OAuth state store + noninteractive auth errors is validated, exporting MCPOAuthStore/MCPOAuthToken/ErrMCPOAuthNoninteractiveRequired.
not_ready_when: The slice opens HTTP listeners, calls real OAuth issuers, ports the full mcp_oauth_manager.py, or wires a managed gateway.
blocked_by: MCP OAuth state store + noninteractive auth errors
acceptance: TestRefreshMCPOAuth_StillValidNoOp: token with ExpiresAt=now.Add(time.Hour) returns Outcome='still_valid' without calling refresher.
TestRefreshMCPOAuth_RefreshesWhenExpired: expired token with non-empty RefreshToken returns Outcome='refreshed' and store now holds the new token from refresher.Refresh.
TestRefreshMCPOAuth_ClearsOnRefresherError: refresher returns ErrMCPOAuthSessionExpired sentinel; store is cleared and Outcome='token_cleared'.

TestRefreshMCPOAuth_NoninteractiveWhenNoRefreshToken: token with empty RefreshToken in noninteractive store returns Outcome='noninteractive_required' and a non-nil ErrMCPOAuthNoninteractiveRequired error.
TestRefreshMCPOAuth_RefresherUnavailable: nil refresher returns Outcome='refresher_unavailable' without panicking and store is not mutated.
items.note: Follow-up to the MCP OAuth state store. Refresh and 401 recovery are isolated as a pure function over a fake refresher so workers do not have to land transport, persistence, and refresh in one row.
done_signal: internal/tools/mcp_oauth_refresh.go declares RefreshMCPOAuth, MCPRefresher, MCPOAuthRefreshResult, and ErrMCPOAuthSessionExpired; five named tests pass; the store from the prior row is reused without modification.

provenance.note: Hermes defines refresh + 401 recovery semantics; Gormes lands them as a pure function over the in-memory store + a fake refresher before any live transport.

items.name: Managed tool gateway bridge
items.contract: Managed tool gateway bridges expose discovered remote tools through the same MCP descriptor/call contract as local fake servers without requiring live external services in tests
items.degraded_mode: Managed gateway status reports gateway_unavailable, auth_required, schema_rejected, and tool_call_failed evidence without registering half-discovered tools.
ready_when: MCP stdio + HTTP transports, MCP schema normalization + structured-content adapter, and MCP OAuth state store + noninteractive auth errors are all validated on main.
not_ready_when: The slice contacts a live managed gateway, performs real OAuth, introduces provider-specific managed tools, or bypasses the MCP descriptor/call normalization layer.

acceptance: A fake managed gateway returns a tool inventory that normalizes through the same descriptor path as MCP fake servers.
Tool calls pass through request IDs, arguments, timeouts, and cancellation to the fake gateway and normalize success/error results.
Auth-required and unavailable gateway responses reuse the MCP OAuth/degraded evidence contracts.
No live external server, credential, or network dependency is required by unit tests.
items.note: TDD: port `tools/managed_tool_gateway.py` only after MCP config, discovery, and OAuth stores are green, proving tool discovery and result pass-through without a live external server.
done_signal: Managed-gateway fixtures prove inventory discovery, pass-through calls, auth/unavailable degradation, and zero live-service dependency over the shared MCP descriptor path.

provenance.note: Hermes defines managed gateway behavior; Gormes ports it after native MCP config/discovery/OAuth contracts.

drift_state.origin_decision: Hermes still leads MCP runtime behavior across stdio/HTTP discovery, OAuth, and managed gateways. Honcho 51497f1 adds self-hosted MCP Worker HONCHO_API_URL config, so Gormes tracks that drift in the first config/env slice while keeping internal Goncho memory local.

items.name: Plugin SDK

Invalid names, unsupported capability kinds, missing required fields, and incompatible version constraints fail closed with structured status evidence.

Project plugin discovery remains disabled unless an explicit config/env gate is present.

Gormes now parses plugin.yaml plus dashboard/manifest.json for identity, version constraints, tool/hook/dashboard/backend-route capabilities, required env/auth, dashboard entry/css/api, tab override/hidden, and slots; malformed manifests, invalid names, unsupported capability kinds, incompatible constraints, missing credentials, disabled execution, and project-plugin gate denial all fail closed with structured evidence. internal/tools records disabled plugin capability rows without registering executable handlers, and internal/apiserver exposes disabled plugin inventory through /api/status and /api/dashboard/plugins without importing or executing plugin runtime code.

items.name: Dangerous action gating
items.note: Umbrella only: do not execute as one slice. Use the detector, approval-mode, cron-mode, and policy-integration tasks below before dangerous terminal/file/code tools become available.

items.name: Hardline command pattern table + DetectHardline function
items.contract: internal/tools declares HardlinePattern{Regex, Description}, a HardlinePatterns []HardlinePattern table seeded from Hermes HARDLINE_PATTERNS, and DetectHardline(cmd string) (matched bool, description string) that compiles patterns once via sync.Once and is pure (no I/O, no globals besides the compiled list)
items.degraded_mode: DetectHardline returns false for any input on a corrupt regex (test-only seam) instead of panicking; production patterns are validated at init.
ready_when: Sandboxed exec is complete; the local sandbox in internal/tools/execute_code.go shows the package conventions to follow.
The slice owns a single new file plus its test; no execute_code.go change, no result struct, no integration with approval modes.
not_ready_when: The slice ports the recoverable DANGEROUS_PATTERNS list, defines a blocked-result struct, edits execute_code.go, or wires the detector into any handler.
The slice introduces approval prompts, network calls, or shells out in tests.
acceptance: TestDetectHardlineRmRoot: 'rm -rf /', 'sudo rm -rf /', 'rm -rf /etc', 'rm -rf $HOME' all match with non-empty descriptions.
TestDetectHardlineMkfsAndDD: 'mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda1', 'dd if=/dev/zero of=/dev/sdb', '> /dev/sda' all match.
TestDetectHardlineForkBomb: ':(){ :|: & };:' matches 'fork bomb'.
TestDetectHardlineShutdownAndReboot: 'reboot', 'shutdown -h now', 'systemctl poweroff', 'init 0', 'telinit 6' all match; 'echo reboot', 'grep shutdown logs', 'systemctl status' do NOT match.
TestDetectHardlineKillAll: 'kill -1', 'sudo kill -1' match; 'kill -9 1234' does not match (recoverable).
DetectHardline returns (false, '') for empty input and for benign commands like 'ls', 'echo hi', 'mkfs_helper'.
items.note: Audit shows the prior 5.J row kept failing as no_progress because the worker had to port HARDLINE_PATTERNS, port DANGEROUS_PATTERNS, design a result schema, and wire execute_code in one slice. Splitting out the hardline-only table makes the first slice unambiguously small. The split intentionally drops the prior health/quarantine block; the contract is materially replaced.
done_signal: internal/tools/dangerous_hardline.go declares the pattern table and DetectHardline; six table tests cover root/home, mkfs/dd, fork bomb, shutdown anchoring, kill all, and benign lookalikes; execute_code.go is unchanged.

provenance.note: Scaffold-first split of the prior quarantined 5.J row. Hardline-only floor mirrors HARDLINE_PATTERNS/_CMDPOS; recoverable patterns and result schema are in the next row.

items.name: Recoverable dangerous patterns + blocked-result schema
items.contract: internal/tools declares DangerousPatterns []HardlinePattern (recoverable rules from Hermes DANGEROUS_PATTERNS), DetectDangerous(cmd) (matched, description), and BlockedResult{Approved=false, Hardline bool, Description, Operator, Command, Evidence map[string]string} produced by GuardCommand(cmd, mode) which calls DetectHardline first then DetectDangerous; pure, no execute_code wiring in this slice
items.degraded_mode: GuardCommand returns BlockedResult{Hardline=true} unconditionally for hardline matches; recoverable dangerous matches return BlockedResult{Hardline=false, ApprovalRequired=true} which yolo-mode callers can ignore in a later slice.
ready_when: Hardline command pattern table + DetectHardline function is validated so DetectHardline can be called from GuardCommand.
The slice owns one new file plus its test; execute_code.go and the registry are not touched in this row.
not_ready_when: The slice modifies execute_code.go, calls execute_code.Sandbox.Execute, prompts for approval, or implements yolo/cron mode toggles.
The slice introduces approval mode config parsing or persistence.
blocked_by: Hardline command pattern table + DetectHardline function
unblocks: Tirith, path, URL, and website policy integration
acceptance: TestGuardCommandHardlineWins: GuardCommand('rm -rf /', any-mode) returns BlockedResult{Hardline=true, Approved=false, Description containing 'root filesystem'}.
TestGuardCommandRecoverable: GuardCommand('rm -rf /tmp/x', mode='manual') returns BlockedResult{Hardline=false, ApprovalRequired=true}; GuardCommand('git reset --hard', mode='manual') likewise.
TestGuardCommandBenign: GuardCommand('ls', mode='manual') returns BlockedResult{} (zero value indicating no block).
TestBlockedResultEvidenceFields: Evidence map records the matched pattern description and the input command verbatim.
TestGuardCommandPure: GuardCommand has no side effects (verified by absence of os/exec, net/http, os.Open in dangerous_command.go).
items.note: Complete: TDD added internal/tools/dangerous_command_test.go and the pure internal/tools/dangerous_command.go guard. The recoverable Hermes DANGEROUS_PATTERNS table compiles under Go regexp, DetectDangerous reports matched descriptions, GuardCommand calls DetectHardline first, hardline results remain unapproved and non-bypassable, recoverable results set ApprovalRequired=true, benign commands return the zero value, evidence records the command and matched pattern description, and execute_code.go remains untouched.
done_signal: GuardCommand returns typed BlockedResult values; hardline beats recoverable; benign returns zero value; no side effects; execute_code.go is still unchanged.

provenance.note: Continuation of the 5.J quarantine split. Defines BlockedResult schema and aggregator that composes DetectHardline with the recoverable table.

items.name: Cron context_from output chaining
items.contract: Cron jobs can reference prior job outputs as bounded prompt context without waiting for same-tick jobs or reading outside the cron output store
items.degraded_mode: Cron execution skips missing, unreadable, invalid, or not-yet-run context sources with visible evidence while preserving the base prompt.

not_ready_when: The slice waits for upstream referenced jobs to finish in the same scheduler tick, reads arbitrary file paths, or changes Phase 2.D cron run audit semantics.

acceptance: Create and update paths normalize context_from as a list of job IDs, preserve it on unrelated updates, and clear it with empty list/string input.
Unknown job IDs are rejected before persistence; tampered path-traversal IDs are ignored at prompt-build time.

Prompt assembly injects the most recent completed output from each referenced job before the job prompt, truncates each output at 8000 characters with a marker, and skips missing/deleted/unreadable outputs without failing the run.
The implementation reuses the Go cron store/run audit or a derived output read model instead of introducing Hermes' Markdown output directory as source of truth.
items.note: Hermes b35d692f added cron job chaining through context_from. Gormes should preserve the behavior over its SQLite/bbolt cron substrate and CRON.md mirror, not by copying Hermes' ~/.hermes/cron/output directory layout.
done_signal: Cron context_from fixtures prove validation, clearing, most-recent-output injection, truncation, and graceful skip behavior over the Go cron store.

provenance.note: Hermes defines the chaining behavior; Gormes maps it onto the native cron store/run audit instead of adopting Hermes' file layout.
